items.title: The 5 Most Islamophobic Tweets From Republicans Since the Iran War Began
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
American wars in the Middle East have always relied on Islamophobic tropes and dehumanization to manufacture consent at home, but the vile, anti-Muslim rhetoric we’re seeing coming from congressional Republicans has reached new levels since Donald Trump launched his illegal war on Iran.

There are five Republicans in particular who, since the Iran war began, have somehow become even more Islamophobic and vicious than they already were. Democrats and civil rights groups have denounced many of them, but Republicans have largely remained silent.

Here are five of the most Islamophobic tweets from Republicans since the Iran war began less than a month ago:

1. Tennessee Rep. Andy Ogles: ‘Muslims don’t belong in American society’

This March 9 tweet from Rep. Andy Ogles was rightly condemned by Democrats and civil rights groups. Guess who refused to condemn it? House Speaker Mike Johnson.

2. Florida Rep. Randy Fine: ‘We need more Islamophobia’

Like Ogles, Rep. Randy Fine has made so many Islamophobic comments in the last three weeks (and his entire congressional career) that it was difficult to choose the worst. But bluntly calling for more Islamophobia, as he did on March 12, certainly meets the bar.

3. Texas Rep. Brandon Gill: ‘Islam is a problem’

Rep. Brandon Gill has long been an open racist, so it should come as no surprise that the Texas congressman hasn’t held back in spewing hateful rhetoric toward Muslims as he did in this March 1 tweet, calling an entire faith, practiced by 2 billion people worldwide, including 4.5 million in the US, “a problem” – while turning “God is Great” in Arabic into a violent reference.

4. Wisconsin Rep. Derrick Van Orden: ‘Radical Muslim sleeper cells’

Then there’s Rep. Derrick Van Orden, who revived an old favorite dog whistle: radical Muslim sleeper cells.

5. Texas Rep. Keith Self: ‘Radical Islam is on the offensive in America’

Rep. Keith Self has focused on another one of the favorite go-to tropes for Islamophobes – creeping Sharia!

The Sharia-Free America Caucus and Its Leaders

These Republicans, of course, are not the only Islamophobic members of Congress – they’re just perhaps among the worst.

The rapidly growing Sharia-Free America Caucus has now reached 59 members who come from 24 states, Self, the group’s co-chair, announced today. This group’s mission is to “save Western Civilization and fight back against the threat of Sharia.”

At its core, this group is pushing for a Muslim ban. “Our immigration system must be prepared to confront this challenge and defend our Judeo-Christian values,” said Congressman Roy.

The Council on American-Islamic Relations (CAIR) designated the caucus an anti-Muslim hate group, the first time the civil rights group has made the designation for a caucus in its 32-year history.

Zeteo’s Ninar Taha contributed to this piece.

items.title: US Troops Are Being Told to ‘Get Ready’ to Invade Iran
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Donald Trump’s administration is moving thousands of US service members to the Middle East East, amid the disastrous US-Israeli war on Iran. And, just as with the historic public opposition to the war, there is unprecedented dissent brewing inside the military – with some troops currently on their way to the Middle East seeking to refuse fighting.

Mike Prysner of the Center on Conscience and War joins Prem to discuss the remarkable levels of dissent inside the military, the fear and intimidation US service members are facing, and how the Trump administration and Israel are to blame for US troops now aiming to leave their posts.

Apparently, soldiers are being told to prepare for the worst. Prysner said directives coming from command to the rank-and-file are stark: “They’re saying a ground war is inevitable, like this is happening, you better get ready.”

High-profile alleged war crimes, including the strike on the Minab girls’ school, as well as the US attack of the Iranian Naval ship returning from an Indian training exercise, have disturbed many US soldiers, Prysner said. He told Prem his organization has seen a “1,000% increase” in new conscientious-objector clients since the start of the war in Iran.

Watch the interview above to hear more details about the dissent brewing inside the military, the rank-and-file disrespect of Trump and Pete Hegseth, and how many more troops may join the “dozens” who already left because of the genocide on Gaza.

The media is either loudly banging the drum for war, or completely disregarding the historic public and military dissent to it. We at Zeteo are reporting on the war like it is. Please subscribe to support us in our mission to build a media fit for the moment.

We are giving you a 5-minute preview of this interview. Become a paid subscriber to watch the whole conversation – and to make sure you have full access to all our content. Thank you!

If you have any tips or information on the war, you can text Prem on Signal @ premthakker.35. You can remain anonymous.

items.title: So Now WE Are Using Human Shields?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1942, the US government began moving Americans of Japanese ancestry into internment camps. Some 84 years later, the Department of Homeland Security is keeping thousands of people in disturbingly parallel conditions, despite increasing public disgust.

Good morning! Prem here. Thinking about contingency. Would the Iraq War have unfolded how it did if the chads weren’t hanging in Florida in 2000? Where would we be if Kamala Harris had said literally anything other than “not a thing comes to mind” when asked if she’d do anything differently than Joe Biden? What would the world look like if someone had given Donald Trump a reality show he stuck with, instead of a political Napoleon complex? And what coin-flips will we look back on with horror, if we don’t see them now?

In today’s ‘First Draft’, I pose to you: are Israeli and US forces using human shields? Plus, Trump is sending ICE agents to airports to “help” the TSA agents he is refusing to pay (a dry run of sending them to the polls in seven months) –and speaking of, the Supreme Court is considering a decision that would stop certain mail-in ballots from being counted.

Here we go…

Human Shield of Lies

For two and a half years, the US and Israeli governments and their media allies all justified the Israeli military’s horrific bombing of hospitals, schools, and homes in Gaza on the grounds that Hamas was hiding its fighters and military infrastructure in civilian areas and, therefore, using Palestinians as “human shields.”

Now, as the US and Israel continue their illegal war on Iran, one could question who is actually using “human shields.”

Months before the war, the US evacuated hundreds of troops from Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar, moving some to hotels in the region. A US official and another source familiar told NBC News this was in order to keep them out of harm’s way. If Palestinian fighters or Iranian soldiers had been moved in such a fashion, you could imagine the US or Israel contending that civilians had actually been put in harm’s way.

In the days leading up to the war, the US was placing refueling tanker airplanes at Israel’s Ben Gurion Airport – the country’s busiest, serving thousands of civilians every day. During the war, it has been used for refueling.

Iranian airstrikes have killed US service members in a “makeshift operations center” at a civilian port in Kuwait and injured US personnel at a hotel in Bahrain. Inside Israel, apartment buildings were destroyed when Iran targeted an Israeli nuclear research center in retaliation for Israeli attacks on Iranian nuclear sites.

It’s unclear why Israeli officials placed nuclear infrastructure near residential homes, or why US officials have positioned US military assets in and around civilian infrastructure.

“We started by attacking their military bases. They have evacuated their military bases and gone to the hotels and made human shields for themselves,” Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Aragchi said, shortly after the US and Israel began the war.

In Israel’s case, it’s structural. As CNN’s Jim Sciutto described two years ago, the headquarters of Mossad, Israel’s international intelligence agency, is in “a densely-populated area” within Tel Aviv, “with civilians around it.”

One could also question whether the spread of US military bases in the region causes unwitting civilians to function as de facto human shields. As Iran has retaliated against US and Israeli attacks, their targets have included US bases, leaving civilians in nations like Qatar and the UAE at risk.

The asymmetry of “human shields” logic reveals how the US and Israel view war. Attacks on Palestinian or Iranian civilian locations are “mistakes” or consequences of their adversaries’ use of human shields. Attacks on US or Israeli civilian locations are definitively “acts of terror.”

Asked about the US bombing of an Iranian girls’ school, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said, “Well, clearly, the United States would not deliberately target a school.”

He continued: “The Iranians are, on the other hand, targeting civilian infrastructure … because you know why? They are a terroristic regime. They sponsor terrorism, and they participate in terrorism.”

In international law, “human shielding” doesn’t mean operating in a civilian area – it requires intent to actively use civiliansagainst their will to deter an attack. The US and Israel have repeatedly failed to provide hard evidence of Hamas doing that. But according to the US-Israeli definition of human shields – putting your fighters inside a civilian building or area – then they themselves are undeniably guilty of using human shields too.

Then again, forget equivalence: a stream of reporting has shown that Israeli forces have literallyusedPalestinians as human shields, in the most brutal of manners. (Remember the wounded Palestinian man that the Israelis strapped to the hood of a military vehicle during a raid in the occupied West Bank city of Jenin in 2024?)

So, to conclude: the US and Israel lied about the definition of human shields to justify Israel’s assault on civilians in Gaza; they then deployed their own soldiers as human shields against Iranian attacks, according to their own definition; and all the while, Israel used actual human shields in both Gaza and the West Bank.

Hypocrisy, thy name is the US and Israel.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s state broadcaster said late Saturday more than 1,500 people had been killed, citing the Health Ministry. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured in US-Israeli strikes.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,039 people, including 118 children, in Lebanon, according toyesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 17, including two Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks in Israel.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinians in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Iran names Larijani replacement: Iran named Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr as the new secretary of the Supreme National Security Council after the previous head, Ali Larijani, was assassinated last week, Iranian state media reported.

Lebanon latest: Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz said today that Israeli forces will control all remaining bridges (meaning those Israel didn’t already blow up) over the Litani River and a “security zone” up to the river. The hundreds of thousands of Lebanese people who fled north of the river will not be allowed to return to their homes “until security for the residents of the north is ensured,” Katz said.

US mulling deploying US combat troops: A combat brigade from the US Army’s 82nd Airborne Division could be deployed to support operations in Iran, defense officials told theNew York Times, which said the “Immediate Response Force” troops could be used to seize Kharg Island, Iran’s main oil export hub.

War aims doubt: Both the US and Israel now see overthrowing the Iranian regime and ending its pursuit of a nuclear weapon as out of reach, according to officials quoted in the Washington Post.

Bet on it: Half a billion dollars’ worth of bets were placed by traders in the oil market about 15 minutes before Donald Trump posted on Monday about “productive” talks with Iran, sending prices tumbling, the FT reported.

‘Hot option’: News that the US had proposed talks between Vice President JD Vance and Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, speaker of the Iranian parliament, prompted one expert to describe “deep-seated Iranian distrust” of Trump peace envoy Steve Witkoff. Politico reported that in the White House, Ghalibaf is seen as “a hot option” to lead Iran.

📊 Chart of the Day

To date, this administration has deported the parents of around 11,000 American citizen children. Check our ProPublica’s full report here.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Voting rights in peril: The Supreme Court appeared skeptical about a Mississippi law that allows mail-in ballots to be counted up to five days after election day, as long as they’re postmarked by that date.

ICE at airports: Donald Trump deployed ICE agents to major US airports, purportedly to “help” the TSA agents he has refused to pay for more than a month. Trump reportedly rejected a GOP-led deal to fund the Department of Homeland Security without ICE on Sunday.

Mullin confirmed: Markwayne Mullin was confirmed as the new DHS secretary, replacing Kristi Noem. GOP Senator Rand Paul, who has accused Mullin of “anger issues,” voted against the confirmation, while two Democrats – Senators John Fetterman and Martin Heinrich – broke with their party to support Mullin’s confirmation.

Voice of Trump: Voice of America journalists are suing Kari Lake – Trump’s pick to lead the US Agency for Global Media (USAGM), which oversees VOA – arguing that she is working to make the network a Pro-Trump propaganda machine.

Fossil fuel funds: The Trump administration has agreed to pay French energy company TotalEnergies nearly $1 billion to ditch plans to produce wind power and instead invest that money in oil and gas.

Mills chilled: Maine Democratic voters are frustrated with Governor Janet Mills for her attack ad against Senate primary rival Graham Platner, over old online comments about victims of rape. In a meeting with Hancock Democrats, one attendee told Mills, “I’m a member of the #MeToo generation, and I was offended by your ad. I’d very much like you to run a clean campaign.” Platner leads most polls.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Voice of America wrecker and former Arizona GOP gubernatorial candidate Kari Lake is one of Donald Trump’s most ardent acolytes. But which candidate for high office did she donate to and campaign for in her home state, Arizona, in 2008?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel’s systematic torture of Palestinians in Israeli prisons and beyond is “state doctrine,” UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese said in a new report. She added: “Since the onset of the genocide, the Israeli prison system has degenerated into a laboratory of calculated cruelty.”

🇭🇺 Trump ally ‘treason’: After the Washington Post reported that PM Viktor Orbán’s far-right Hungarian government has for years provided Russia with information about EU Council meetings, opposition leader Péter Magyar, who is leading in the polls three weeks before election day, said: “If confirmed, this would amount to treason, which carries a potential life sentence.”

🇮🇹 Trump ally beaten: Italy PM Giorgia Meloni suffered a rare electoral defeat in a referendum on controversial justice system reforms, the public rejecting the far-right leader’s proposal by around 54%-46%. Meloni has said she won’t resign over the result, but analysts said it could leave her significantly weakened.

🇰🇵 North Korea vow: Leader Kim Jong Un said his country’s nuclear-armed status was “irreversible,” adding that Pyongyang would “expand and advance our self-defensive nuclear deterrent.”

🇨🇴 Colombia crash: A military transport plane with 128 people on board came down shortly after take-off in a remote Amazonian part of the southwest of the country, authorities said, killing at least 66 dead and injuring dozens of others.

🧠 Trivia answer: Barack Obama.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

items.title: So Now WE Are Using Human Shields?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo

Where would we be if Kamala Harris had said literally anything other than “not a thing comes to mind” when asked if she’d do anything differently than Joe Biden? And what coin-flips will we look back on with horror, if we don’t see them now?

Plus, Trump is sending ICE agents to airports to “help” the TSA agents he is refusing to pay (a dry run of sending them to the polls in seven months) –and speaking of, the Supreme Court is considering a decision that would stop certain mail-in ballots from being counted.

items.title: Less Than 1% of Public Bomb Shelters in Israel Are in Palestinian Communities
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
In response to Israel’s (now usual) bombing of foreign countries like Lebanon and Iran, there is usually some sort of (expected and natural) response. Two questions are immediately posed inside Israel: 1) Do you support Israel’s bombing of Country X or Y, and 2) Do you feel “safe”? It is in response to these two questions that you can see just how different Israeli and Palestinian lives are, as well as their perceptions of the world.

For Jewish Israelis, an overwhelming (and by overwhelming, I mean overwhelming) majority support Israel’s latest strikes on Iran, just as they support every Israeli attack on every country and on Palestinians, always. In a survey by the Israel Democracy Institute, a whopping 92.5% of Jewish Israelis support the attack on Iran, including 68% of those who self-identify as “left wing” in Israel. By contrast, Palestinian citizens of Israel largely do not support this or any other Israeli military attack, with roughly 25% saying they support Israel’s strikes on Iran. This same survey asked whether people in Israel feel protected from Iranian attacks: 79% of Jewish Israelis surveyed feel protected compared to 15% of Palestinian citizens of Israel.

Why the differences? Simple. Because Palestinians in Israel have never been part of Israeli society, yet we pay the price for Israel’s wars. (Incidentally, no one ever bothers to poll Palestinians in the occupied West Bank – including Jerusalem – or in the Gaza Strip, and they too pay a price for Israel’s assaults.)

Only 37 Shelters

Let’s begin by looking at the issue of “safety” and feeling “protected.”

Of the 11,775 public shelters in Israel, only 37 – yes 37 (!) – are based in Palestinian-majority towns, according to a recent government study. That means only 0.3% of Israel’s public shelters are allocated to 20% of the citizens of the state. Even after last year’s June attack on Iran, Israel did nothing to rectify this gross inequality.

In my own city, Haifa, often described (by non-Palestinians, of course) as a model of “coexistence,” we see the same picture. During the Nakba, Israel ethnically cleansed 95% of Haifa’s 75,000 Palestinian residents, with only 3,000 remaining. Those who remained were forced into the Wadi Nisnas neighborhood. Today, this neighborhood is home to about 9,000 Palestinians, yet their own public shelter is a single school gym.

The city of Nof HaGalil was built on the land stolen from Nazareth’s residents. It has 24 public shelters for its 45,000 residents, whereas Nazareth, the largest Palestinian city in Israel, with its 80,000 residents, has none. I think about my own family in Nazareth and the elderly in Haifa, wondering how they “stay safe” as we are always told to do.

And remember, there are no shelters at all in Gaza or in the West Bank. Fragments of an Iranian missile killed four Palestinian women, including a teenager and two pregnant women, who were at a hair salon preparing for Eid near Hebron. They had no warning; no shelter to go to.

Israel Will Massacre at Will

The way in which Palestinians view the war versus Israelis isn’t just an issue of shelters; it’s an issue of ideology. Palestinians do not support Israel’s wars because we know how Israel was founded: by ethnic cleansing, by massacres, and by theft. How else can someone have the audacity to live in the home of another (literally), sleep in their bed, eat off their plates, and pretend that it is just? That’s what happened in 1948. Fast forward to 2026, and how can they continue to live in our houses or on our stolen land while also perpetrating genocide? The answer is that it can only be done through a very deep process of dehumanization. And we, Palestinians, have been on the receiving end of that dehumanization for as long as Israel has been around. To be a Palestinian in Israel is to largely live among Nakba perpetrators, apologists, and/or advocates. Today, it also means to largely live among genocide perpetrators, apologists, and/or advocates. We do not want to see Israel do to Lebanon or Iran what it did (and continues to do) to us. And, unlike many Western leaders who downplay the biblical references to mass killings and land grabs uttered by Israeli leaders and ministers, we Palestinians believe them. Because they are true. Israel will massacre at will – as it always has; and steal more land – as it always has.

For example, in the aftermath of Israel’s bombing campaign in Lebanon in 2006, the Israelis began using the phrase the “Dahiyeh doctrine,” named after the Dahiyeh suburb of Beirut that was massively bombed by Israel, which hit residential buildings, without regard for civilians, and targeted power plants, buildings, and other infrastructure to collectively punish Lebanon (it continues to strike Dahiyeh today). This, of course, is illegal. Today, Israel no longer refers to the Dahiyeh doctrine because it has amped up its crimes; there is a new standard. Israel’s Finance Minister, Bezalel Smotrich, has promised that Beirut will look like Khan Younis, while Israeli “Defense” Minister Israel Katz has promised that it will look like Rafah and Beit Hanoun. It should be remembered that Rafah and Beit Hanoun have been completely destroyed – not a fully intact building remains – while more than 80% of Khan Younis has been destroyed. It is also the site of a mass grave. So the Dahiyeh doctrine is replaced by the Rafah doctrine. This is Israel’s new standard.

And so we are opposed to the endless wars. But should we dare raise our voices in opposition, the racist, fascist (masked as “democratic”) state comes in to put us in place. Since the start of Israel’s attack, Palestinians have been arrested for social media posts, for allegedly spray-painting a Palestinian flag on government buildings (Israel’s chief inciter and National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir personally attended the arrest of one man in an obviously made-for-TV moment), and for having a Palestinian flag in their homes. Incidentally, with Zionist aggression comes Zionist fragility. To be a Palestinian living in any part of Palestine is not only about navigating how to simply survive, but also navigating Zionist violence and Zionist attempts to always show their strength, while instead showing that they can and will commit war crimes. This Zionist violence is not just during times of war but on a daily basis – because that is what Zionism is – violent – and we are its first victims, though there will be many, many more. So, no, we are not “safe” and do not and will not ever be duped into the belief that perpetrating wars and war crimes will make anyone safer.

Diana Buttu is a Haifa-based lawyer and analyst who was a legal adviser to the Palestinian negotiating team in the early 2000s and is a frequent commentator and writer on Palestinian and Israeli issues. She is also a practitioner in residence at Georgetown University in Qatar. She writes Zeteo’s ‘A Diary from a Palestinian in Israel.’

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.

items.title: 🇮🇷 The Kharg Island Bloodbath?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 2022, US Secretary of State Antony Blinken formally declared that members of Russia’s armed forces had committed war crimes in Ukraine. Four years on, Donald Trump initially said Iran had until Monday evening to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, or he would “obliterate” power plants – an act generally considered a war crime itself. He said this morning he was postponing any strikes on Iran’s power plants for five days.

Good morning! Swin and Andrew here, with an update on Donald Trump’s illegal, pointless, disastrous, and expanding war in Iran. Our idiot president has, for decades, wanted to “go in and take” Kharg Island and seize Iran’s oil infrastructure – and because he’s surrounded himself with cultists and sycophants in his second term, he’ll get to carry out his fantasy, if that’s what he chooses.

The only problem? Well, many Americans would likely die pointlessly.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the Trump administration knows many Americans will die if he invades Kharg Island, Trump and Iran exchange dire threats to life-sustaining infrastructure, and ICE is heading to America’s airports.

A ‘Considerable’ Body Count

Senior Trump administration officials have been briefed on US casualty estimates – prepared by military and intelligence personnel – for a variety of possible ground invasion scenarios in their illegal Iran war, two sources familiar with the situation and another person briefed on it tell us.

In multiple scenarios, if Donald Trump were to invade and try to occupy Kharg Island for an extended period of time, American casualties would very likely be “considerable,” one of the people familiar with the matter says. Some estimates, the first two sources tell us, are easily in the dozens for an initial phase of occupation, with numbers likely to rise sharply if President Trump wished to hold the island for an extended duration.

Some of the internal Kharg analysis has been shared directly with Trump in recent weeks, sources stress.

Trump has long been fascinated with Kharg Island, which holds the bulk of Iran’s oil exports. During his Art of the Deal book tour in 1988, Trump said he would “go in and take it.”

Harrison Mann, a former US intelligence analyst, has written forZeteo about why a Kharg Island invasion is a bad idea. He writes further for the Quincy Institute’s Responsible Statecraft blog: “For the troops unlucky enough to receive orders to take Kharg, the operation would land somewhere between a suicide mission and a self-imposed hostage crisis.”

As he explains, it’s not clear what the point of the Kharg mission would be, because securing Iran’s oil infrastructure might not necessarily create much leverage for Trump. “The opportunity to inflict a mass casualty event that could sap limited US public support for the war, or to hold entire battalions as de facto hostages, may well appear more valuable to Tehran than oil revenue,” Mann adds.

Joe Kent, Trump’s former director of counterterrorism, who just resigned over Trump’s Iran war, told the Washington Post that a Kharg invasion “would be a disaster.”

“It would essentially be giving Iran a bunch of hostages on an island that they could barrage with drones and missiles,” he said.

One thing was obvious to us lately: A good number of Zeteo’s well-placed sources, in and close to the US government, are leaking things like this to us (and, we’re sure, others in the media and elsewhere) because they do not want the president to pull the trigger on this. They say they think a ground invasion, whether protracted or not, would be an inevitable bloodbath and political disaster for the American side.

We will be straight with you: We do not think their efforts to spook President Trump out of this are going to work. We really hope we are wrong.

The president has moved roughly 9,000 troops and Marines to the Middle East, and is only threatening to escalate further. On Saturday, Trump said that if Iran “doesn’t FULLY OPEN, WITHOUT THREAT, the Strait of Hormuz,” where Iran is blocking much of the world’s oil supply, by Monday, “the United States of America will hit and obliterate their various POWER PLANTS, STARTING WITH THE BIGGEST ONE FIRST!”

Early Monday, Trump said on his social media site that he had postponed any military strikes on Iran’s power plants and energy infrastructure for five days, claiming the US and Iran have had “VERY GOOD AND PRODUCTIVE CONVERSATIONS” over the last two days “REGARDING A COMPLETE AND TOTAL RESOLUTION OF OUR HOSTILITIES IN THE MIDDLE EAST.” He did not elaborate.

Iran has threatened to respond to any attacks on its power plants with even bigger attacks on all kinds of infrastructure around the Gulf.

By many indications, Trump’s illegal war in Iran – a baseless war of choice never authorized by Congress – will expand. Its threat to people’s lives and livelihoods is growing.

An array of Trump advisers and other sources with intimate knowledge of the situation say that while the president could vacillate on this rapidly (as he does with plenty of things), at this time, the chances of a US invasion of Iran in the near future remain dangerously, stubbornly high.

“This is already an unpopular war being led by an unpopular president,” says Matt Duss, a former top foreign policy adviser to Bernie Sanders. “A US ground invasion, and the resulting US casualties, will make it even more unpopular, in addition to a strategic disaster. The fact that Trump seems to think he can escalate his way out of this mess is astonishing. He needs to pull the plug on this catastrophe now.”

Right now, you shouldn’t hold your breath.

But at least Fox host Sean Hannity and Senator Lindsey Graham are happy.

Graham, the South Carolina Republican senator who helped quarterback Trump into this war, is giddy about the Kharg Island idea.

Speaking on ‘Fox News Sunday,’ he compared it to the World War II battle with Japan over Iwo Jima – a fight in which 6,900 US soldiers were killed, and 19,000 were injured.

“I trust DOD. We have two Marine Expeditionary Units sailing to this island. We did Iwo Jima – we can do this,” Graham said.

He added, “I don’t know if you take it or block it, but I know this: The day we control that island, this terrorist regime has been weakened, it will die on a vine.”

We will all see what happens. But as it stands: President Trump is THIS CLOSE to deploying ground troops in yet another war in the Middle East, with absolutely no clear mission, and potentially no end in sight. And he’s weighing doing this with all the care and respect for human life that a toddler would apply to throwing dad’s car keys down the toilet.

Any American – or anyone anywhere else on the planet – should be horrified. We’ll stay on this story every day of the week here at Zeteo.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s state broadcaster said late Saturday more than 1,500 people had been killed, citing the Health Ministry. Iranian officials said earlier this month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured in US-Israeli strikes. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency put the death toll at more than 3,000, including 1,407 civilian deaths.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,029 people, including more than 115 children, in Lebanon, according tofigures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 17, including two soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks in Israel. An Israeli man was killed Sunday in what Israel eventually admitted was a friendly fire incident in the northern part of the country.

Occupied West Bank: Four Palestinian women were killed in an Iranian missile attack in the West Bank.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region.

And in more Iran war news…

Iran’s warning: Tehran said power plant strikes would lead to the complete closure of the Strait of Hormuz and strikes to “irreversibly destroy” Middle East infrastructure, including energy and desalination plants. The country’s National Defence Council later warned “all communication lines in the Persian Gulf” would be mined if a land invasion takes place.

Nuclear fears: On Saturday, after Israel hit Iran’s main nuclear enrichment site at Natanz, Iran struck Dimona and Arad, close to nuclear research facilities in Israel’s south, injuring around 200. Iran also unsuccessfully attacked the US-UK base at Diego Garcia in the Chagos Islands, beyond what was thought to be its maximum missile range.

Internet blackout among ‘most severe’ ever: Iran’s near-total internet blackout is now in its 24th day, internet advocacy group Netblocks said today, calling it one of “the most severe registered in any country.”

Lebanon bridge blast: Defense Minister Israel Katz said he had ordered more destruction of bridges and houses in the south of Lebanon, as part of Israel’s war against Hezbollah. Israel then bombed a bridge near Tyre, prompting Lebanese President Joseph Aoun to warn of a “prelude to ground invasion.” The bombing of bridges will have significant consequences for getting humanitarian aid to the southern part of the country.

Worse than 1970s oil shocks combined: The head of the International Energy Agency warned today that the oil crisis caused by the US-Israeli war on Iran was worse than the 1973 and 1979 oil shocks combined. The global economy faces a “major, major threat” and “no country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction,” Fatih Birol said at Australia’s National Press Club today.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Yes men: Right-wing media baron Rupert Murdoch and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu were the key voices in driving Donald Trump to strike Iran, sources told Bloomberg. Where were close advisers like Vice President JD Vance or Secretary of State Marco Rubio? They were silent, afraid of stepping in Trump’s way.

DHS vs. Abrego Garcia: The Trump administration is continuing its push to deport Kilmar Abrego Garcia, with the Department of Homeland Security asking a federal judge to remove an order blocking his deportation. The agency says it is gearing up to remove Abrego Garcia in an “extremely expeditious manner” and send him to Liberia, to which he has no connection.

ICE at the airport: Border czar Tom Homan says ICE agents will be deployed to airports starting today, to assist TSA workers with security lines as the partial government shutdown continues. Trump announced the move over the weekend, in an effort to put pressure on Democrats to fund ICE along with the rest of the Department of Homeland Security.

Pete’s (failed) attack on the press: In a win for press freedom, a federal judge ruled against the Pentagon policy restricting press access for news outlets producing coverage the department dislikes. Judge Paul Friedman called out the agency for seeking “only stories that are favorable to or spoon-fed by department leadership,” and to “weed out disfavored journalists”.

Noem’s successor: Senator Markwayne Mullin is on course to lead the Department of Homeland Security, after his Senate colleagues voted 54-37 to advance his nomination to a final vote, perhaps as early as today. Two Democratic senators voted for him – John Fetterman of Pennsylvania (OF COURSE!) and Martin Heinrich of New Mexico.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

In 2014, Senator Martin Heinrich spent six days on a desert island with a GOP senator for Rival Survival, a Discovery Channel special, emerging “sunburned, bug-bitten, banged up, and a few pounds lighter – but alive.” Who was the Republican?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

💬 Quote Unquote

“In essence, we are Jiu-Jitsu-ing the Iranians. We are using their own oil against them.”

That’s Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, in response to being asked why the US lifted sanctions on Iranian oil, thereby essentially helping fund a country it’s at war with. Yeah, nice try Scott!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: In its latest violations of the so-called “ceasefire,” Israel killed four people on Sunday, Palestinian officials said, including three police officers killed at Nuseirat refugee camp and a senior figure of an armed group killed in Sheikh Radwan.

🇵🇸 Another night of settler violence: Israeli settlers rampaged through Palestinian villages and communities in the occupied West Bank for a second night on Sunday, injuring at least nine Palestinians and torching homes and cars, local media reported.

🇬🇧 London Jewish community ambulances set on fire: Authorities in London said four ambulances belonging to a Jewish community organization were set on fire overnight in what investigators are treating as an antisemitic hate crime. No injuries were reported.

🇰🇪 Kenya amnesty: Kenyans enlisted to fight for Russia against Ukraine will be granted amnesty upon returning home, Prime Cabinet Secretary Musalia Mudavadi said after visiting Moscow for talks. According to Ukrainian intelligence, more than 1,700 Africans have been lured to Russia with promises of lucrative employment, then sent to fight.

🇫🇷 Paris elects socialist: Emmanuel Grégoire was elected mayor of the French capital, defeating the conservative Rachida Dati. A far-right candidate became mayor of Nice, France’s fifth-largest city, but Marine Le Pen’s National Rally party was defeated in many other major cities.

🇸🇩 Sudan strike: At least 64 people, including 13 children, were killed in a strike on a hospital in western Darfur, the World Health Organization said over the weekend. Officials with the Sudanese military, which has been fighting the Rapid Support Forces paramilitary group since 2023, reportedly claimed the strike was meant to hit a nearby police station.

📊 Chart of the Day

Make no mistake.

While this war is wildly unpopular, and some would argue it’s splitting the MAGA movement, the president has still managed to maintain the vast majority of his pro-Trump cult, who’ll support just about anything he does – regardless of what Tucker Carlson or Candace Owens might say!

🧠 Trivia answer: Senator Jeff Flake of Arizona.

📺 Sunday Show Roundup

For whatever reason, NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte seems to be kissing up to Trump, telling Fox he thinks the war on Iran is “necessary” and praising the “president’s leadership” on the issue of Iran’s nuclear capabilities.

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy is openly admitting that the real reason ICE is being deployed to airports is to force Democrats to concede on the issue of funding the whole DHS. Duffy told ABC’s Jonathan Karl he believes Trump is seeking to take “leverage” away from Democrats, and said ICE agents know how to perform TSA job responsibilities, even when reports show they are barely trained.

Confronted with Trump’s shameless response to the death of Robert Mueller, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent tried to make Trump the victim. “Given what has been done to President Trump and his family, it is impossible for either of us to understand what he’s been through,” Bessent told NBC, when asked if it was appropriate for a president to celebrate the death of any American citizen.

Asked on NBC if Trump was “winding down this war” with Iran or escalating it, Bessent went full ‘Veep’ (or was it ‘In the Loop’?) when he said: “Again, they’re not mutually exclusive. Sometimes you have to escalate to de-escalate.” The world awaits a formal response from Armando Iannucci – or Simon Foster MP.

GOP senator Thom Tillis, a vocal critic of the Trump administration, stood by his comments calling out Kristi Noem’s lack of qualifications to be Homeland Security Secretary, but stopped short of blaming Trump himself for her appointment. Speaking to ABC, Tillis said he didn’t expect the president to have an “encyclopedic” knowledge of all his Cabinet picks, but has questions around how Noem came to be nominated.

🗓️Mark Your Calendars

Monday, March 23: The Supreme Court is set to hear arguments in Watson v. RNC, a case challenging a Mississippi law that allows mail-in ballots sent by Election Day to count, even if they arrive later.

Tuesday, March 24: A US District Court judge is set to weigh Anthropic’s bid to undo Pentagon blacklisting.

Wednesday, March 25: The three-day 2026 CPAC summit is set to begin in Dallas.

Thursday, March 26: Venezuela’s abducted President Nicolás Maduro is due back in court in his US narco-terrorism case.

Saturday, March 28: The third national ‘No Kings’ protest is set to take place across the country.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly, Akshay Gokul, and Alexa Cohen contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

items.title: 🇮🇷 The Kharg Island Bloodbath?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 2022, US Secretary of State Antony Blinken formally declared that members of Russia’s armed forces had committed war crimes in Ukraine. Four years on, Donald Trump initially said Iran had until Monday evening to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, or he would “obliterate” power plants – an act generally considered a war crime itself. He said this morning he was postponing any strikes on Iran’s power plants for five days.

Good morning! Swin and Andrew here, with an update on Donald Trump’s illegal, pointless, disastrous, and expanding war in Iran. Our idiot president has, for decades, wanted to “go in and take” Kharg Island and seize Iran’s oil infrastructure – and because he’s surrounded himself with cultists and sycophants in his second term, he’ll get to carry out his fantasy, if that’s what he chooses.

The only problem? Well, many Americans would likely die pointlessly.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the Trump administration knows many Americans will die if he invades Kharg Island, Trump and Iran exchange dire threats to life-sustaining infrastructure, and ICE is heading to America’s airports.

A ‘Considerable’ Body Count

Senior Trump administration officials have been briefed on US casualty estimates – prepared by military and intelligence personnel – for a variety of possible ground invasion scenarios in their illegal Iran war, two sources familiar with the situation and another person briefed on it tell us.

In multiple scenarios, if Donald Trump were to invade and try to occupy Kharg Island for an extended period of time, American casualties would very likely be “considerable,” one of the people familiar with the matter says. Some estimates, the first two sources tell us, are easily in the dozens for an initial phase of occupation, with numbers likely to rise sharply if President Trump wished to hold the island for an extended duration.

Some of the internal Kharg analysis has been shared directly with Trump in recent weeks, sources stress.

Trump has long been fascinated with Kharg Island, which holds the bulk of Iran’s oil exports. During his Art of the Deal book tour in 1988, Trump said he would “go in and take it.”

Harrison Mann, a former US intelligence analyst, has written forZeteo about why a Kharg Island invasion is a bad idea. He writes further for the Quincy Institute’s Responsible Statecraft blog: “For the troops unlucky enough to receive orders to take Kharg, the operation would land somewhere between a suicide mission and a self-imposed hostage crisis.”

As he explains, it’s not clear what the point of the Kharg mission would be, because securing Iran’s oil infrastructure might not necessarily create much leverage for Trump. “The opportunity to inflict a mass casualty event that could sap limited US public support for the war, or to hold entire battalions as de facto hostages, may well appear more valuable to Tehran than oil revenue,” Mann adds.

Joe Kent, Trump’s former director of counterterrorism, who just resigned over Trump’s Iran war, told the Washington Post that a Kharg invasion “would be a disaster.”

“It would essentially be giving Iran a bunch of hostages on an island that they could barrage with drones and missiles,” he said.

One thing was obvious to us lately: A good number of Zeteo’s well-placed sources, in and close to the US government, are leaking things like this to us (and, we’re sure, others in the media and elsewhere) because they do not want the president to pull the trigger on this. They say they think a ground invasion, whether protracted or not, would be an inevitable bloodbath and political disaster for the American side.

We will be straight with you: We do not think their efforts to spook President Trump out of this are going to work. We really hope we are wrong.

The president has moved roughly 9,000 troops and Marines to the Middle East, and is only threatening to escalate further. On Saturday, Trump said that if Iran “doesn’t FULLY OPEN, WITHOUT THREAT, the Strait of Hormuz,” where Iran is blocking much of the world’s oil supply, by Monday, “the United States of America will hit and obliterate their various POWER PLANTS, STARTING WITH THE BIGGEST ONE FIRST!”

Early Monday, Trump said on his social media site that he had postponed any military strikes on Iran’s power plants and energy infrastructure for five days, claiming the US and Iran have had “VERY GOOD AND PRODUCTIVE CONVERSATIONS” over the last two days “REGARDING A COMPLETE AND TOTAL RESOLUTION OF OUR HOSTILITIES IN THE MIDDLE EAST.” He did not elaborate.

Iran has threatened to respond to any attacks on its power plants with even bigger attacks on all kinds of infrastructure around the Gulf.

By many indications, Trump’s illegal war in Iran – a baseless war of choice never authorized by Congress – will expand. Its threat to people’s lives and livelihoods is growing.

The fact that Trump seems to think he can escalate his way out of this mess is astonishing. He needs to pull the plug on this catastrophe now.”

He added, “I don’t know if you take it or block it, but I know this: The day we control that island, this terrorist regime has been weakened, it will die on a vine.”

We will all see what happens. But as it stands: President Trump is THIS CLOSE to deploying ground troops in yet another war in the Middle East, with absolutely no clear mission, and potentially no end in sight. items.title: 7 Things You Need to Know About the Strait of Hormuz – That Trump Didn’t!
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Donald Trump is digging himself deeper and deeper into a war he seems to know nothing about. So when Iran shut down the Strait of Hormuz – a threat the US has known about for years – Trump was, of course, unprepared. But it was a predictable move to the officials who briefed him… or to anyone who has looked at history, or at a map, ever.

Now, the tiny waterway is at the very center of this war, has spiked oil and gas prices globally, and is sending both developed and developing worlds into an economic crisis.

So, here are 7 things you should know about the Strait of Hormuz (that Trump probably didn’t!)...

1. Where it is on a map…

Bordered by Iran to the north and Oman and the UAE to the south, the Strait of Hormuz connects the oil-rich Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea. It’s only 24 miles wide at its narrowest point. Shipping is restricted to a pair of two-mile-wide lanes: one outgoing, one incoming. It’s the only passage out of the Gulf to the rest of the ocean.

2. It’s the world’s most important waterway

The strait facilitates over one fifth of the world’s oil shipments. In fact, this war is causing the world’s largest oil supply disruption in history. Thanks to geography and political tension, there’s no real alternative to the strait. Try as they might, Saudi and UAE pipelines can only carry a small fraction of the energy, and even then, are vulnerable to attacks from insurgent groups like the Yemeni Houthis.

Besides the US, China, India, and Japan also heavily depend on the oil that passes through the strait. Even if this block on oil tankers is only temporary, it will raise global energy prices, spike shipping costs, cause major supply delays, and could even lead to a worldwide food shortage – Hormuz is a crucial shipping route for fertilizers needed now for planting season.

3. “Opening the Strait of Hormuz” is virtually impossible

Trump is on a mission to forcefully “open” the strait, begging world leaders for help. He, and people like Lindsey Graham, are very “angry” that pretty much every country replied: “We’d rather not get involved, sir.” Now, the US seems to have bullied some of those countries into submission and is bombing targets around the strait, supposedly to wipe out anti-ship missiles.

But there is effectively no way to secure the strait. The short area, with shipping lanes only 3 miles from the Iranian shore in some places, allows Iran to launch missiles and drones from its coast, with complete fire control over the entire strait. The risk is too high for the US navy to enter, or even to escort commercial ships – Iran could easily hit them.

Experts say it could be difficult for Iran to blockade the strait long term, but Iran has effectively closed the waterway since the war began by firing at oil tankers and reportedly laying mines.

4. The decline of the US empire?

We’ve seen this movie before…

In 1956, Britain, France, and Israel attacked Egypt after President Gamal Abdel Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal – a critical chokepoint in Egypt for much of Europe’s oil supply. The US and Egypt staved off Britain and France’s overreach, and the failure marked the decline of British and French imperial power.

If the US loses this fight, experts say it could permanently shatter the nation’s credibility as a superpower.

Could this be Trump’s ‘Suez moment’?

5. Just how badly the Hormuz closure could hit the global economy

According to Trump? “We don’t need oil. We have all the oil we need for ourselves.”

Meanwhile, US gas prices have surged to the highest they’ve been in years, the US has eased sanctions on Iranian oil, and Trump is threatening to strike Iran’s power plants. Sounds like we really need oil. That’s because oil prices are set on a global market, and countries across the Middle East choked off oil production by at least 10 million barrels a day – that’s about 10% of global supply, according to the I.E.A.

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz is the “nightmare scenario” for the global energy system.

6. The US knew this would happen

Of course, Trump also said he knew this would happen all along in that bizarre and rambling way of his: “I knew about the strait, that it would be a weapon, which I predicted a long time ago. I predicted all of this stuff… I predicted Osama bin Laden would knock out the World Trade Center…”

Trump’s supposed precognitive gifts aside (he did not in fact predict Bin Laden would hit the WTC!) – the US has known for years that Iran could mine the Strait of Hormuz and use it as a threat. President Trump was warned that Iran would likely respond this way. He still went ahead and launched this war.

Plus, Iran has used the strait as a pressure point before when officials threatened a Hormuz shutdown in 2019 after Trump stopped waiving sanctions for Iranian oil importers.

7. Can the US win this battle?

For the US to win, experts say a ground invasion might be necessary. Former intelligence analyst Harrison Mann wrote that reopening shipping lanes would look like “an indefinite occupation” and “a full-fledged invasion” of the Iranian coast. To fend off missiles, drones, and clear out mines, fully reopening the strait could take a month and tens of thousands of troops. And even if Iran is significantly weakened, insurgents like the Houthis could attack ships in the strait instead.

The world’s least competent president strikes yet again… but this time, the world’s energy supply and global power politics are on the line.

Zeteo’s Layla Nayfeh contributed to this piece.

Check out more from Zeteo:
items.description: Donald Trump is digging himself deeper and deeper into a war he seems to know nothing about. So when Iran shut down the Strait of Hormuz – a threat the US has known about for years – Trump was, of course, unprepared. But it was a predictable move to the officials who briefed him… or to anyone who…

items.title:
This Week in Palestine: Israel Kills 4 Members of One Family and Attacks
 Muslim Worshippers on Eid
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
As the US-Israeli war rages in Iran and Israel escalates its bombardment of Lebanon, Israel’s genocide in Palestine continues.

Just this week, Israeli forces shot dead four members of one family whose only crime was being Palestinian while driving home at night after dinner in the occupied West Bank. Israel also killed more than a dozen Palestinians across Gaza; we learned just how dangerous the US-Israeli war on Iran can be for Palestinians in the West Bank; and Israeli settlers carried out a night of violence, setting fires to cars and homes and beating Palestinians.

The Iran war, high oil prices, and Donald Trump’s idiocy have turned away what little attention there was on the ongoing genocide in Palestine, but as we promised, Zeteo will not become distracted. That’s why we started ‘This Week in Palestine’ – bringing some of the biggest and most underreported stories from across the Occupied Palestinian Territories.

A quick note before we jump into this week: For now, we’re sending ‘This Week in Palestine’ to all Zeteo subscribers, but that won’t always be the case. To ensure you get the newsletter every week, go to ‘manage subscription’ in your Substack account, scroll down, and toggle on the button for ‘This Week in Palestine’. Please make sure you’re a paid Zeteo subscriber so you can read past the paywall.

Now, here’s ‘This Week in Palestine’:

Saturday, March 14 –Israeli Forces Kill 4 Members of Palestinian Family

Israeli forces shot dead Ali Bani Odeh, his wife, Waad, their blind 6-year-old son, Othman, and their youngest son, Muhammad, in their car as they returned home from dinner in the occupied West Bank late Saturday night. The couple’s two eldest sons, Khaled, 11, and Mustafa, 8, survived the attack, with Khaled telling reporters: “My mother let out a cry and then she went silent and I did not hear my brothers.” He added that Israeli soldiers beat him and his brother and said, “We killed dogs” when they reached the car.

As it often does, Israel attempted to paint the family as a threat to justify the shooting, but its comments were quickly dispelled by the accounts of the two surviving brothers and other witnesses.

Sunday, March 15 –93% of Gaza’s Cemeteries Destroyed

Israel has systematically destroyed more than 93% of Gaza’s cemeteries since Oct. 7, 2023, the Euro-Med Human Rights Monitor advocacy group said in a report.

The group said that the widespread destruction shows Israel is carrying out “a deliberate, planned effort to obstruct the identification and documentation of remains and to disrupt any subsequent procedures for identification, examination, or lawful exhumation.”

Monday, March 16 – Israeli Forces Kill 16-Year-Old Boy

Israeli forces shot and killed 16-year-old Salim Sami Salmi Fuqaha – the seventh Palestinian child killed by Israel in the occupied West Bank this year, according to Defense for Children International - Palestine (DCIP), a local children’s rights group.

Israeli forces opened fire on Salim and 15-year-old Fathi Ali Fathi Al-Sahouri and then prevented paramedics from reaching the boys for hours. Al-Shouri survived the attack, per DCIP.

Tuesday, March 17 – Israel Is Carrying Out ‘Mass Expulsion’ of Palestinians in West Bank

Israel forcibly displaced more than 36,000 Palestinians across the occupied West Bank in one year (from November 2024 through October 2025) in what UN experts warned was the “mass expulsion of Palestinians on a scale previously unseen, amounting to unlawful transfer that is prohibited under international humanitarian law.”

The UN experts’ reportalso documented 1,732 incidents of settler violence against Palestinians –up from 1,400 incidents in the previous reporting period.

Wednesday, March 18 - Four Palestinians in the West Bank Killed in the Iran War

Four Palestinian women, including a teenager, were killed when falling debris or part of an Iranian munition hit a hair salon near Hebron in the occupied West Bank. The women are the first known Palestinian fatalities in the West Bank related to the US-Israeli war on Iran.

Palestinians in the West Bank do not have the same kinds of warning systems or shelters that Israelis have.

Thursday, March 19 –Aid Allowed Into Gaza Plunges 80%

Haaretz, citing the US-led Civil-Military Coordination Center, reported that the amount of aid Israel has allowed into Gaza since the start of the war on Iran has plunged 80%.

Prior to the Iran war, a weekly average of 4,200 trucks – still not near enough for the need – were allowed into Gaza. Only 590 trucks were allowed to enter in the first week of the war. The number increased to 1,137 trucks during the second week.

Friday, March 20 - Israeli Forces Attack Muslim Worshippers in Jerusalem on Eid

For the first time since 1967, the Al-Aqsa Mosque Compound, home to the third-holiest site in Islam, was closed at the end of Ramadan, forcing worshippers to perform Eid prayers on the street.

Israel closed the site early in its war on Iran, citing security reasons, but Palestinians warn Israel is using the excuse as a pretext to further tighten its control over the holy site.

Ahead of Friday prayers, Israeli police attacked worshippers gathered outside Jerusalem’s Old City.

Saturday, March 21 - Israeli settlers rampage through Palestinians villages

Israeli settlers rampaged through at least six areas in the occupied West Bank, torching Palestinian homes and cars, damaging other property, and beating residents. At least Palestinians were hospitalized after the attacks.

No arrests were reported.

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more recent essays and interviews from Zeteo:

Just this week, Israeli forces shot dead four members of one family whose only crime was being Palestinian while driving home at night after dinner in the occupied West…

items.title: ‘I’m Glad He’s Dead’: Trump Spits on Robert Mueller’s Grave
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
On Saturday afternoon, President Donald Trump took a break from weighing a ground invasion of Iran, so that he could dance on the grave of former FBI Director and Special Counsel Robert Mueller.

“Robert Mueller just died. Good, I’m glad he’s dead. He can no longer hurt innocent people!” he wrote on his social media site, before signing his post, “President DONALD J. TRUMP,” so that nobody could be confused about the official nature of the proclamation. One of the White House’s verified Twitter accounts dutifully shared Trump’s post.

Trump and the GOP have been furious with the late special counsel, who was a registered Republican, ever since the president’s first term, when Mueller led the federal investigation into Trump and his associates over Russia’s efforts to interfere in the 2016 election.

Zeteo emailed the White House to ask whether it would be appropriate for other politicians to react this way if they were to hear news of Trump’s passing. (We all know how the Trump-Vance administration responded after Charlie Kirk’s assassination.)

The White House did not immediately respond to our request for comment.

This type of conduct is a frequent occurrence from the supposed leader of the free world. In December, for example, President Trump giddily mocked filmmaker Rob Reiner, upon news of his brutal killing, and naturally made it all about himself.

“Rob Reiner, a tortured and struggling, but once very talented movie director and comedy star, has passed away, together with his wife, Michele, reportedly due to the anger he caused others through his massive, unyielding, and incurable affliction with a mind crippling disease known as TRUMP DERANGEMENT SYNDROME, sometimes referred to as TDS,” Trump posted on social media.

Check out more from Zeteo:

Good, I’m glad he’s dead. He can no longer hurt innocent people!” he wrote on…

items.title: How US Imperialism Shaped the Iran We See Today
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
On February 28, the political landscape of the Middle East shifted dramatically following a reported US airstrike that killed Ayatollah Khamenei. But 2026 is far from the first time the US has been involved in Iranian politics.

Many of the sentiments and strategies we see today echo actions from over 70 years ago in 1953, when a democratically elected leader was overthrown after attempting to nationalize Iran’s oil. For many Iranians, this moment feels like part of a much longer pattern of Western intervention shaping the country’s trajectory. If you think you know the story, let’s take a closer look at how the past and present are deeply connected.

Check out more from Zeteo:

But 2026 is far from the first time the US has been involved in Iranian politics. Many of the sentiments and strategies we see today echo actions from…

items.title: Trump’s White House Is Screaming at Me That Americans Love His Awful War
items.author: Mehdi from Zeteo
items.content:
Here at Zeteo, we thought we could perhaps get through one work week without Donald Trump’s White House – once again! – calling me a “moron” for publicly stating objectively true things.

We were wrong. What was my crime this time?

On Thursday, Trump’s press secretary, Karoline Leavitt, took to social media to claim that “Americans Agree that Operation Epic Fury Is an Overwhelming Success” – meaning the president’s disastrous war in Iran.

I pointed out that the senior Trump apparatchik was lying, and had in fact shared “an article showing only MAGA Republicans support the war.”

In response to this, “@RapidResponse47,” a Trump White House propaganda account on Twitter blasted me as a “moron” and suggested I was “trembling too much at the idea that Americans support killing terrorists.”

Of course, that’s not what’s happening – civilians are suffering as a result of Trump’s regional bloodbath, and poll after poll shows this war is uniquely unpopular at home.

And the White House made clear how unpopular the war is with its own press release. In it, the administration pointed people to a relatively rosy Washington Post article that still notes more people “would prefer that the strikes stop than continue,” and that the paper’s average of recent polls show “Americans opposing US strikes 50% to 38%.”

Not exactly a ringing endorsement!

The Iran war – started by Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu – is now entering its fourth week. It has been an unmitigated disaster from the beginning, and it’s strangling the global economy. The president appears to be preparing to deploy US ground troops to Iran, for a mission that looks like sheer suicide.

As we’ve reported at Zeteo, the war is starting to tear apart the MAGA elite, and is already a political catastrophe at home for Trump and the Republican Party – and they know it. And America’s allies have decided not to join us on this so-called “excursion.”

The president and his party want to silence anyone in the media willing to admit what a colossal shitshow this all is. We refuse to be silent.

In recent months, the Trump White House has called Zeteo “a fake news blogsite run by left-wing hacks that no one should take seriously.”

The White House issued a statement calling one of our political correspondents “a deranged hack who couldn’t even hold a job at Rolling Stone and is now writing for a Greta Thunberg blog.”

And the Trump administration routinely comes after me, and tries to smear me as a terrorist-loving “moron.”

Clearly, Zeteo is getting under their skin.

But the only way we can fight back is together.

So if you’re able, please consider becoming apaid subscriber to Zeteo today to support us and help grow independent media. If you’re already a paid subscriber (thank you kindly!), you can also consider making a donation to Zeteo.

Every paid subscription, every donation, every financial contribution, big or small, sends a message to this authoritarian, warmongering administration and this country’s ruling party and its enablers that we won’t be bullied, we won’t be silenced, and we won’t stop telling the truth about fascism, genocide, illegal wars, and other moral and literal atrocities.

The Trump administration wants to wear out everyone – in the media, in the opposition, in the electorate. They aren’t getting tired yet. Neither are we.

Thank you for standing with Zeteo.

We were wrong. What was my crime this time? On Thursday, Trump’s press secretary, Karoline Leavitt, took to…

items.title: American Idiot Holds World Hostage
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 2003, the Bush administration launched a ground invasion of Iraq. The US is supposed to finally fully withdraw from Iraq later this year, over two decades later, which might make you wonder: Why would Donald Trump launch a war in Iran?

Good morning! Andrew here to discuss what was a pretty normal day in Donald Trump’s second term: The president of the United States joked about Pearl Harbor with Japan’s prime minister and also made a not-so-subtle nuke threat, as his illegal war in Iran inches closer and closer to spiraling fully out of control.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Trump embarrasses the US again, as his war gets more expensive for everyone; the Department of Homeland Security keeps trying to steal employees from the Pentagon; and muted Eid and Nowruz celebrations are held across the Middle East as the US-Israeli war on Iran rages.

Our Mad King, Global Edition

“Why didn’t you tell US allies in Europe and Asia, like Japan, about the war before attacking Iran?” a reporter asked Donald Trump on Thursday, as the president sat in the Oval Office with Japan’s prime minister.

Trump, the man with no filters, no restraint, no sense of decorum, responded by joking about… wait for it… Pearl Harbor. “We didn’t tell anybody about it because we wanted surprise,” he said. “Who knows better about surprise than Japan? Why didn’t you tell me about Pearl Harbor? … You believe in surprise, I think much more so than us.”

The comments made Japan’s right-wing leader, Sanae Takaichi, visibly uncomfortable – while also confirming that Trump’s administration didn’t inform our allies before launching his illegal war in Iran, a conflict with disastrous implications for the global economy.

Moments like this, in the Oval Office, have become routine under America’s first Mad King. Last summer, Trump joked to Germany’s new chancellor, Friedrich Merz, that D-Day was “not a pleasant day for you.” Merz quickly corrected him, explaining: “In the long run, Mr. President, this was the liberation of my country from Nazi dictatorship.”

But Trump isn’t just embarrassing the US on the global stage, or mocking key allies on camera. He’s spent the past 14 months attacking, threatening, and alienating them with his tariffs.

And with the US-Israeli attack on Iran, Trump has taken our allies along for the ride as he wages what is already the worst-run war in US history, and as the conflict generates blowback of all kinds, both around the Middle East and throughout the world.

And the blowback was entirely predictable.

Trump claimed this week that “nobody” knew Iran would respond to his war by attacking other countries in the Gulf. Citing “the greatest experts,” Trump said none of them thought Iran would target what he described as “neutral” countries nearby.

But just days before Trump attacked Iran, Nate Swanson, a former National Security Council official who served on Trump’s Iran negotiating team last year, published a piece in Foreign Affairs warning of this exact scenario. “If conflict with the United States deepens, Iran may seriously consider targeting the Gulf Arab states’ energy infrastructure directly,” he wrote.

Similarly, the US always knew that if it launched major attacks on Iran, the country would likely retaliate by targeting ships in the Strait of Hormuz to shut down the crucial waterway, choke off the flow of oil, and send energy prices skyrocketing. Trump was briefed on this likelihood before launching the war – but he ignored and downplayed the warnings.

The president and his team have spent much of the past week publicly begging our allies to team up with him on a dangerous mission to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. By and large, they all said no thanks. Trump finally got a few countries to issue a joint statement indicating their interest in ensuring “safe passage through the strait” – but no commitments to send ships or the minesweepers he actively demanded.

On Wednesday, Israel escalated the economic situation by striking Iran’s South Pars gas field – the largest in the world. Iran responded by attacking Qatar’s gas facilities – taking out 17% of Qatar’s liquefied natural gas export capacity – and also launched missiles at Saudi Arabia.

Trump claimed in a social media post that the United States “knew nothing about this particular attack” beforehand. Several media outlets quickly reported that this was a lie. Nevertheless, Trump also threatened to “massively blow up the entirety of the South Pars Gas Field” if Iran continued to attack Qatar.

Trump’s illegal war on Iran has already sent gasoline prices soaring in the US and around the world. This latest phase, in which countries exchange attacks on nations’ essential energy infrastructure, could cause long-term economic damage: Prices are likely to spike and remain high for years after the conflict ultimately ends. Oil and gas infrastructure can’t be easily or rapidly rebuilt.

And there’s no sign the conflict will end soon, as Trump seems as eager as ever to escalate. On Thursday, he issued a vague, ominous threat that doesn’t necessarily require a genius to parse: “The military equipment, the power of some of this weaponry, is unthinkable,” he said. “You don’t even want to know about it. You could end this thing in two seconds if you wanted to.”

So… nukes?

Trump’s catastrophic war – which began with a shocking massacre of Iranian schoolchildren – is only going to get worse and more costly for everyone, regardless of their involvement or which side they are on.

Donald Trump might be America’s Mad King, but he is now the whole world’s problem.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iranian officials said last week that at least 1,444 people have been killed and nearly 20,000 injured in the US-Israeli strikes – the latest updated figures from the country. The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency put the death toll at more than 3,000, including over 1,394 civilian deaths.

Lebanon: Israel has killed at least 1,001 people, including more than 115 children, in Lebanon, according toyesterday’s figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 17, including two soldiers, have been killed and thousands of others injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks in Israel.

West Bank: At least four Palestinian women were killed in an Iranian missile attack.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed.

And in more Iran war news…

IRGC spokesperson killed: An Israeli strike killed Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) spokesperson General Ali Mohammad Naeini, Iranian and Israeli officials confirmed.

US plane hit: An F-35 jet on a combat mission was forced to make an emergency landing after it was apparently hit by enemy fire, sources told CNN. If confirmed, it would be the first case of a US plane being damaged by Iranian air defenses.

No way to reopen Strait: European powers do not see a way to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the key waterway to commercial shipping, without ending the war, Bloomberg reported.

US eyes Iranian oil: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Fox the US might “unsanction the Iranian oil that’s on the water,” adding that “about 140 million barrels” could be made available.

Israeli strike targets reporter: RT correspondent Steve Sweeney narrowly survived an Israeli strike in southern Lebanon – in an attack caught on camera. Sweeney, who, along with his crew, was wounded, said he had “absolutely no doubt that this was deliberate,” adding: “As we have seen in Gaza, they want to silence journalists who document and report their war crimes.” Russia summoned the Israeli ambassador over the strike.

Muted Eid al-Fitr and Nowruz celebrations: In the Gulf, several countries suspended large outdoor gatherings typically held, restricting prayers to mosques. In Palestine, Israel blocked Muslim worshippers from the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound, which has been closed since the early days of the war. In Iran, celebrations for both Eid and the Persian New Year, Nowruz, came amid US-Israeli bombs.

DHS Raids the Pentagon

Amid Trump’s illegal and costly war in Iran, the Pentagon sent out an email Thursday to civilian employees, encouraging them to volunteer for jobs at the Department of Homeland Security in service of Trump’s war at home.

“Many of our dedicated civil servants have already answered the call to defend the homeland; 900 have submitted applications to answer President Trump’s call for support to immigration enforcement activities that enhance public safety,” Pentagon official Timothy Dill wrote in the email, which was obtained by Zeteo.

Dill promised that employees who make the jump to DHS will “leave work each day knowing you have made your country safer.”

Let me be the first to say it: No, you won’t.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

WTF Fetterman?! The Senate is set to vote on the confirmation of GOP Senator Markwayne Mullin as DHS secretary. It is all but certain to pass, following a fiery two-day committee hearing that ended in GOP Senator Rand Paul and Democrats voting against the nomination. Mullin squeezed through because Senator John Fetterman abandoned fellow Democrats to vote in his favor.

Inhumane ICE: 19-year old Royer Perez-Jimenez died at an ICE detention center in Miami that has a record of medical neglect, with ICE claiming the cause of his death was “presumed suicide.” Perez-Jimenez is the youngest known fatality in ICE custody as the death toll in detention centers reaches a two-decade high under the Trump administration.

‘Political retribution’: FBI Director Kash Patel was sued for allegedly firing two former FBI agents, in violation of their First and Fifth Amendment rights, because they investigated Trump’s efforts to overturn the 2020 election. At a congressional hearing, Patel, pressed on his termination of agents who investigated Trump, claimed they violated “ethical obligations.”

Illegal deportations: An ICE officer revealed that DHS has deported more than 100 asylum seekers who were supposed to be protected by a court-ordered settlement. It’s the latest development in a legal battle over Trump’s administration illegally deporting an individual to El Salvador’s notorious megaprison CECOT.

Warren backs Platner: Senator Elizabeth Warren threw her support behind progressive oyster farmer and veteran Graham Platner in the Maine Senate Democratic primary, as tensions heighten between him and his opponent, Governor Janet Mills.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

IMEU Policy Project, Demand Progress, and Data For Progress conducted a poll to gauge US voters’ latest opinions on the war with Iran. What percentage of voters said they believe that the war benefits Israel more?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel allowed only eight injured Palestinians and 17 of their family members to leave Gaza through the Rafah border crossing yesterday after reopening it for the first time in weeks.

🇹🇳 Activist jailed: Saadia Mosbah, a prominent Tunisian migrant rights and anti-racist activist detained since May 2024, was sentenced to eight years in prison on charges of money laundering and illicit enrichment. Her lawyer said she was a scapegoat for “the state’s failure to address” the issue of African migrants seeking to reach Europe.

🇸🇩 Sudan-Chad border tensions escalate: The president of Chad, Idris Mahamat Déby, ordered military retaliation against future attacks from Sudan, after at least 17 people were killed in a drone attack on a funeral. The Sudanese government accuses Chad of supporting Rapid Support Forces, a paramilitary group that has fought Sudan’s military since April 2023.

😬 WTF?!

The scolds at the centrist think tank Third Way are now arguing it’s antisemitic to say the phrase “Epstein class” – claiming the term, though a recent invention, pushes “age-old tropes about Jewish wealth and influence.”

Good luck getting anyone to buy this one!

🧠 Trivia answer: 56%.

📖 Weekend Reads

JD Vance Is Having Kamala Harris Problems

It’s a political tale that has become familiar in modern politics – a vice president sidelined in crucial decisions, presidential ambitions curbed by an aging boss. In this New York magazine piece, Ross Barkan breaks down how JD Vance’s vice presidency is being marred by the same kind of power struggles Kamala Harris faced in Joe Biden’s White House.

Grieving Parents in Iran Spend Every Night at the Graves of Their Children, Killed by US Strike

In this sobering Drop Site News piece, Mahmoud Aslan shares the stories of the Iranian parents whose children were killed in the US strike on an elementary school in Minab, Iran, with one parent saying, “Ramadan this year arrived carrying a grief I have never known before.”

Transportation Lobbyists Have Donated Thousands to Sean Duffy’s Son-in-Law as He Runs for Congress

Why would someone advocating for a tunnel between New Jersey and Manhattan want anything to do with a Wisconsin congressional race? Well, maybe there’s a candidate who knows a guy (in the White House, conveniently). In this ProPublica investigation, Megan O’Matz reveals the lobbyists trying to get Trump’s transportation secretary’s attention through his son-in-law’s congressional campaign.

Jury Finds Afroman Songs Mocking Cops Too Funny to Be Illegal

Rapper Afroman took the internet by storm this week as he confronted a defamation lawsuit posed by local cops over rap videos calling them out for raiding his home. Read Jay Willis’s Balls and Strikes piece detailing the events that led to Afroman’s fight in court and why the legal battle has folks celebrating him as a free speech hero.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly, Akshay Gokul, and Lara Strydom contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.
