But Judge Don Willett wrote a thoughtful concurrence, joined by Judge Cory Wilson, expressing doubts about the correctness of that precedent.

Avila v. Bondi. Agreeing with the Fifth Circuit, a divided panel of the Eighth Circuit held, in an opinion by Judge Bobby Shepherd, that the Trump administration can arrest undocumented immigrants inside the U.S. and hold them without bond. Judge Ralph Erickson dissented, noting that “[f]ive presidential administrations, including the first Trump administration, and most immigration judges” interpreted the statute at issue contrary to the administration’s current view.

Woolard v. Thurmond. From John Ross’s (delightfully concise) description of this case for Short Circuit: “in en banc news, the Ninth Circuit (over dissentals) will not reconsider its decision that California parents who homeschool their kids—by means of enrolling them in a public charter school program that pays for educational materials—cannot have the state pay for religious educational materials.” Judge Patrick Bumatay wrote one dissent from the denial of rehearing, which four of his colleagues joined, and Judge Lawrence VanDyke wrote the other dissental, which two other judges joined. (This was not a case about “swinging dicks,” and the opinions were appropriate for the whole family.)

Anthropic PBC v. U.S. Department of War. Judge Rita Lin (N.D. Cal.) temporarily blocked the Pentagon’s designation of Anthropic as a national security supply-chain risk (but stayed her ruling to give the administration time to appeal).

Motsinger v. Cosby. A jury in Los Angeles Superior Court awarded $59.25 million in damages to Donna Motsinger, a former waitress who accused disgraced actor and comedian Bill Cosby of drugging and sexually assaulting her.

Litigation of the Week: Watson v. Republican National Committee.

Last Monday, the U.S. Supreme Court heard oral argument in Watson v. Republican National Committee (RNC). Watson concerns whether federal election-day statutes preempt state laws that allow the counting of ballots that are cast on or before Election Day, but not received by election officials until sometime after that day.

Watson challenges a Mississippi law that permits mail-in ballots to be counted as long as they’re postmarked by, then received within five business days of, Election Day. But as noted by Amy Howe of SCOTUSblog, “more than a dozen states have similar laws, [so] the Court’s ruling—which is expected by late June or early July—could have significant implications for federal elections, beginning as soon as November.”

What’s the likely outcome? According to Howe, as well as most of the commentaries collected at How Appealing, it appears the Court will reject the Mississippi law—i.e., a majority of justices will hold that ballots must be received on or before Election Day in order to count—and some state officials are already preparing for that outcome. For more on Watson, I refer you to Sarah Isgur and David French of Advisory Opinions.

In other litigation news:

The quartet of law firms targeted by executive orders from the Trump administration—Jenner & Block, Perkins Coie, Susman Godfrey, and WilmerHale—came out swinging in their D.C. Circuit briefs, opposing the administration’s appeals of its four defeats in the district courts.

Barry J. Pollack, counsel to former Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro, claims that the Trump administration is preventing his client from accessing the money he needs to fund his legal defense—and while Judge Alvin Hellerstein (S.D.N.Y.) declined to dismiss the criminal case against Maduro for that reason, His Honor expressed displeasure with the administration’s actions.

The Roman Catholic Diocese of Albany reached a $148 million settlement with hundreds of victims of child sexual abuse (who could sue thanks to a 2019 New York State law, the Child Victims Act, that allowed adults who were sexually abused as children to file claims that were previously time-barred).

Represented by Boies Schiller Flexner and Edwards Henderson, Jeffrey Epstein victims secured a $72.5 million settlement from Bank of America, which allegedly facilitated his sex-trafficking crimes by providing him with banking services (after JPMorgan Chase cut him off).

Joshua Spriestersbach, 54, will receive a $975,000 settlement from the city and county of Honolulu, based on how he was wrongfully arrested and held for more than two years at a psychiatric hospital in Hawaii. He might also get a $200,000 settlement from the state of Hawaii—whose public defender’s office allegedly contributed to the situation. It’s a pretty crazy story (gift link).

Deal of the Week: the $22 billion merger of Corebridge Financial and Equitable Holdings.

Two financial-services firms, Corebridge Financial and Equitable Holdings, announced their plan to combine through a $22 billion merger, which they touted as creating a leader in the retirement, life insurance, and asset management industries. The law firms on the deal are Skadden Arps for Corebridge and Paul Weiss for Equitable.

In non-M&A deal news, the legal AI giant Harvey completed another funding round, raising $200 million—at a total valuation of $11 billion. While it’s certainly possible we’re going through an AI bubble, it’s not quite like the dot-com bubble of the late 1990s, when companies with little to no revenue or customers received ridiculous valuations. Take Harvey: it boasts many paying clients—like my latest podcast guest Jed Bergman, who said that he and his colleagues at Glenn Agre Bergman & Fuentes are always looking for new use cases—and Harvey’s annual revenue is “significantly north of $200 million,” according to CEO Winston Weinberg.

Law Firm of the Week: Freshfields.

For most of my years covering Biglaw, the so-called “Magic Circle” firms—yes, I know they don’t love that moniker, but it’s useful—struggled to gain traction in the U.S. legal market. Despite their undeniable prestige, impeccable pedigrees, and incredible global reach, these U.K.-founded firms couldn’t seem to succeed on our shores, even as they thrived in continental Europe, Asia, and other markets.

But at some point in the past decade, that changed—and now these firms are major players in the States. Case in point: Freshfields.

The firm was involved in at least two billion-dollar deals announced last week. The smaller transaction, the French food conglomerate Danone’s $1.2 billion acquisition of the British meal-replacement brand Huel, was a classic Magic Circle deal: a cross-border transaction with at least one U.K. party. But Freshfields is also advising Terns Pharmaceuticals, a California-based biotech company, in its $6.7 billion sale to Merck—a purely domestic deal, and one that any number of Am Law 100 firms would love to be involved with. (Covington & Burling represents Merck.)

What allowed Freshfields to take its U.S. practice to the next level? A key factor was its willingness to pay top dollar for top talent, a step the Magic Circle firms had been unwilling to take for years—which brings us to the next big news out of Freshfields.

As reported by Bloomberg Law, Freshfields overhauled its partner compensation system, and now its U.S. partners can earn as much as $17 million a year. In addition, it added a non-equity tier to its partnership—a step taken by many other firms seeking greater flexibility in how they pay their partners, especially the biggest rainmakers.

To be sure, $17 million is far from the top of the market in partner pay—and I wouldn’t be surprised if Freshfields ends up having to cough up more, at least if it wants to hang on to high-profile hires like Ethan Klingsberg (from Cleary) and Damien Zoubek and Jenny Hochenberg (from Cravath—and, by the way, the lead partners on the Merck/Terns deal). For now, however, a willingness to pay partners north of $15 million—even if it might get a few noses out of joint back in London—makes clear that Freshfields is selling top legal talent (and not organic milk at Whole Foods).

In other firm news:

Speaking of being willing to pay up for top talent, Susman Godfrey announced enhanced clerkship bonuses (as one excited reader related to me): $180,000 for one federal district or appellate clerkship, and $200,000 for two or more clerkships. In addition, the firm announced what it described as an “applicant-first summer program,” which you can read about in this press release. It’s similar to that of Munger Tolles & Olson, which I previously praised—and I’m a fan of Susman’s new approach, for all the same reasons.

Speaking of elite litigation boutiques, this year Wilkinson Stekloff celebrated its tenth anniversary—and was the subject of two glowing profiles, in Bloomberg Law and The American Lawyer.

I don’t know what to make of this lawsuit filed in New York state court against White & Case, making claims of discrimination, retaliation, and a hostile work environment. The anonymous plaintiff, who worked for the firm as a digital production specialist, alleges in his complaint that he “was stripped naked and photographed by his supervisor, and potentially other colleagues, while unconscious during a firm retreat”—and that the resulting photographs were then circulated within the firm. A White & Case representative told Law.com that “these claims are baseless, and we intend to defend them vigorously through the appropriate process.” It will be interesting to see how this turns out: on the one hand, the allegations are hard to believe, and the firm’s denial is aggressive; on the other hand, there should be forensic evidence to support them if true (assuming it hasn’t been destroyed, a possibility raised by the plaintiff’s counsel, Eric Baum of Eisenberg & Baum).

Taylor Duma, founded in Atlanta in 2005, will be closing its doors. It grew to more than 150 lawyers as of 2023—but after key partners defected over the past two years, the remaining partners made “the very hard decision to shut down the firm,” as managing partner Marc Taylor told Law.com.

Move of the Week: the NAACP hiring Kristen Clarke as its general counsel.

Kristen Clarke, former head of the DOJ Civil Rights Division in the Biden administration, is no stranger to controversy. But you know who’s also not afraid to ruffle some feathers? The NAACP, which describes itself as “the home of grassroots activism for civil rights and social justice,” prepared to “advocate, agitate, and litigate for the civil rights due to Black America.”

So I wasn’t surprised when the NAACP, one of the nation’s oldest and most well-known civil-rights groups, announced its hiring of Clarke as general counsel (succeeding Janette McCarthy Wallace, who will remain at the organization but transition into a special-counsel role). In her new role, Clarke will oversee NAACP strategy and operations, and she will also head up its efforts on issues like voter access, gerrymandering, and the First Amendment.

Other notable moves:

In New York, HSF Kramer brought over a trio of M&A partners—Robert Leung, Mike Huang, and Daniel Grossman—from Paul Hastings. They had been at Paul Hastings since March 2017, after joining from Boies Schiller Flexner (when the litigation-focused firm spun off what had been its small corporate practice, a move I wrote about in Above the Law).

Down in D.C., another group of three partners—litigators Archis Parasharami, Daniel Jones, and Kevin Ranlett—moved from Mayer Brown to Skadden Arps. Their practice focuses on class actions and mass arbitrations.

Staying in the nation’s capital, Greenberg Traurig hired former Maryland attorney general Douglas Gansler as co-chair of its nationwide state attorneys general practice. He came over from Cadwalader, along with Stephen Ruckman (with whom he worked when he was Maryland AG).

Back in the Big Apple, Allison Liff, who co-led the leveraged finance practice at Freshfields, jumped to Ropes & Gray.

And then we came to the end—of another edition of Judicial Notice. I wish you a good week, as well as a happy Passover and Easter (in advance, as applicable). And to my fellow denizens of the New York tri-state area, where we have some rainy days ahead, stay dry—and always carry an umbrella, even if it’s not cloudy when you leave home.
items.description: I spent last week trying to get back into the swing of things after vacation. It was a low-key week—and after a hectic start to 2026, I welcomed it. Even after a productive week, though, I’m still playing catch-up on many things in my life, both professional and personal—and as a result, I’m doing…

items.title: Judicial Notice (03.29.26): Contempt All Around
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
I spent last week trying to get back into the swing of things after vacation. It was a low-key week—and after a hectic start to 2026, I welcomed it.

Even after a productive week, though, I’m still playing catch-up on many things in my life, both professional and personal—and as a result, I’m doing what I call “exercising my ‘no’ muscle.” I’ve always been someone who tries to say “yes” as much as possible. But this caused me to overextend myself—and by the time I left for vacation, I was burned out. So for now, I’m setting some boundaries and saying “no” to most requests (with the exception of speaking engagements in fabulous destinations).

Before shifting into “no” mode, I accepted an invitation to join Tim Kowal and Jeff Lewis on the California Appellate Law Podcast—and I’m glad I did, since I enjoyed our conversation. I also agreed to participate in a New York City Bar Association webinar about accelerated Biglaw recruiting of law students, which yielded media mentions for me in Law.com articles about (1) five-figure stipends being paid to 1Ls during recruiting and (2) whether the whole process has turned into a “race to the bottom.”

Now, on to the news.

Lawyer of the Week: Robert Frazer.

Here in New Jersey, we’re used to being mocked—much of the time, unfairly—by people from other states. But some recent jokes about my former office, the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of New Jersey (D.N.J.), have been justified. Things over at 970 Broad Street in Newark have been a bit of a mess over the past year.

One of Donald Trump’s former personal lawyers, Alina Habba, led the office for a time. After her term expired, the judges of the district selected a successor—Desiree Grace, an experienced prosecutor—but the Trump administration fired Grace immediately, reinstalling Habba. After Habba’s new appointment was held unlawful by Chief Judge Matthew Brann (M.D. Pa.), whose ruling was affirmed by the Third Circuit, a triumvirate of lawyers—Ari Fontecchio, Jordan Fox, and Philip Lamparello—assumed leadership of the office. But Judge Brann rejected this arrangement as well, and it probably would have met the same fate in the Third Circuit.

Last Monday, Chief Judge Renée Bumb (D.N.J.) announced that she and her colleagues had appointed a new U.S. attorney: Robert Frazer, who has served in the office for 20 years. The Trump administration didn’t fire Frazer—because, as explained in a letter to Judge Brann by supervisory assistant U.S. attorney Mark Coyne, Frazer’s selection “followed consultations between the District Court and the Department of Justice’s senior leadership.” And New Jersey’s two Democratic senators, Cory Booker and Andy Kim, issued a statement that praised the picking of Frazer (while getting in some digs at the Trump administration).

A veteran prosecutor, Frazer has handled more than 700 cases during his two decades in the office, as reported by The New York Times. He’s highly respected both within the office and the defense bar, with D.N.J. alumni turned defense lawyers like Henry Klingeman and Charlie McKenna praising Frazer to the New Jersey Law Journal. He graduated from Brooklyn Law School in 1991, previously worked in the Manhattan District Attorney’s Office, and joined the U.S. Attorney’s Office in 2006.

A number of D.N.J. defendants have challenged their prosecutions by questioning the lawfulness of the leadership structure at the U.S. Attorney’s Office. So the office will seek superseding indictments, signed by Frazer, to dispel any doubts.

I wish Robert Frazer a long and successful tenure in his new role. Based on his excellent reputation, I’m guessing that if the Trump administration were to ask Frazer to do anything he viewed as unethical or unlawful, he’d resign. But given all the drama of the past year, as well as all the other things on its plate, hopefully the Trump administration will leave Jersey alone—at least for now.

Other lawyers in the news:

Why did Margaret “Meg” Ryan resign as director of the enforcement division of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)? As reported by Reuters, she “clashed with agency leaders over the direction of its enforcement program, including the handling of cases with ties to Donald Trump and his family.”

Colin McDonald was confirmed by the Senate, 52-47, to serve as assistant attorney general for the Justice Department’s new fraud enforcement division.

Speaking of AAGs, Harmeet Dhillon has received some criticism for her leadership of the DOJ Civil Rights Division. But she stands by her record—which she vigorously and enthusiastically defended, in an interview with Tunku Varadarajan of The Wall Street Journal (gift link).

The WSJ also had this interesting profile (gift link) of Mark Lanier, the prominent plaintiff’s lawyer who won one of two verdicts against leading social-media companies (discussed below as Rulings of the Week).

Speaking of leading litigators, but moving from the civil to the criminal side, David Oscar Markus got to cross-examine Secretary of State Marco Rubio. Markus represents Esther Nuhfer, one of two defendants in a federal corruption case now being tried in Miami (S.D. Fla.); the other defendant, former congressman David Rivera, is a longtime friend and former roommate of Secretary Rubio.

Another top trial lawyer, Alex Spiro of Quinn Emanuel—the go-to lawyer for Elon Musk, the richest person in the world—had a busy week. On Tuesday, Spiro filed a motion asking for Chancellor Kathaleen McCormick to recuse herself from shareholder litigation against Musk, based on her allegedly clicking to “support” a LinkedIn post critical of Musk (discussed in more detail below).

Then on Thursday, Spiro sent a detailed letter to Judge Charles Breyer (N.D. Cal.), alleging that jurors who found against Musk in a recent securities-fraud trial were biased against his client—and let it show, by making a “420” joke on the verdict form. As explained by Reuters, “[t]he number 420 is associated with marijuana culture,” and Musk “has often mentioned 420 in interviews and tweets, and used it in business activities”—e.g., buying Twitter for $54.20 per share. (Having Musk as a client isn’t easy: on the same day that Spiro sent his letter to Judge Breyer, Musk took to Twitter to mock the judge’s penchant for bowties.)

By now, many of you have seen the viral video featuring Rob Hopkins, a transgender lawyer in Oklahoma, getting arrested for contempt of court in the middle of a hearing. For an analysis of what went down in legal terms, including a discussion of the jurisdictional issues being argued, see this Twitter thread.

In memoriam: Daniel Krisch, an appellate litigator and partner in the Hartford office of Harris Beach Murtha, passed away at 52. May he rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Chancellor Kathaleen McCormick.

Lawyers can be skittish about social media. But LinkedIn, full of anodyne posts of attorneys congratulating their colleagues (or themselves) for getting recognized by this or that ranking, tends to be a “safe space.”

Alas, LinkedIn might not be safe enough—at least not for Chancellor Kathaleen McCormick of the Delaware Court of Chancery. As mentioned above, Elon Musk filed a motion asking Chancellor McCormick to recuse from shareholder litigation against him, based on her apparently “supporting” a LinkedIn post that mocked Musk and his lawyers after their loss in a securities-fraud trial in California federal court. The news was covered by Bloomberg Law, Law.com, Reuters, and even The New York Post.

Chancellor McCormick responded to the motion in a two-page letter. She claimed that (1) she does not actually “support” the post; (2) she either didn’t click the “support” icon, or she did so accidentally; (3) she “reported the suspicious activity to LinkedIn”; and (4) when she tried to log into her account to check the status of her suspicious-activity report, she found that her account was locked.

This is rather… strange. Accidentally “liking” a LinkedIn post, which is the default reaction, is easy. But as explained in Musk’s motion, expressing “support” is harder to do by mistake, since it requires the user to hover over “like,” slide over to the “support” icon, and then click. So if Chancellor McCormick didn’t do it, who did?

(I don’t believe McCormick has young children—but if she does, or even if she has young nieces or nephews, I’d bring them in for questioning. I once let Harlan, who was five at the time, play with my phone for a few minutes, while I was trying to work—and before I knew it, he had ordered $107.68 in Japanese food on DoorDash.)

My guess is that Chancellor McCormick will deny the motion—and, if so, Spiro and Musk will try to appeal. Denial of a recusal motion isn’t a final order—it’s an interlocutory order, issued in the middle of a case—so there’s no automatic right to appeal. But given the high profile of this litigation, this could be the rare case where the Delaware Supreme Court gets involved, either because McCormick certifies an interlocutory appeal or because the Delaware high court issues some type of extraordinary writ (e.g., a writ of mandamus or prohibition). Stay tuned.

In other news about judges and judging:

Donald Trump continues to criticize the judiciary. In a speech at a Republican fundraising event on Wednesday, Trump lashed out at “rogue judges,” calling them “criminals” whose rulings are “hurt[ing] our country.”

In light of such attacks, the desire of judges to defend themselves—and to push back against the Trump administration—is entirely understandable. But when it comes to their judicial opinions, are some judges taking anti-Trump rhetoric too far? Quite possibly, according to judges and legal commentators who spoke with Mattathias Schwartz of The Times (gift link).

If judges want to make themselves heard, they can do so outside their opinions. A new advisory opinion from the Judicial Conference of the United States provides that judges may make “public statements about the need for judicial security and funding,” as well as “the independence of the judiciary [and] the rule of law in general, including why both values are crucial to our system of government.”

Speaking of the Judicial Conference, its Judicial Conduct and Disability Committee rejected Judge Pauline Newman’s challenge to her (seemingly endless) suspension from the Federal Circuit. For news coverage and a link to the decision, see Howard Bashman’s How Appealing.

Why was Chief Judge Kimberly Moore so intent on getting Judge Newman off the bench? One theory I’ve heard is that Judge Moore, who takes a more aggrandized view of her chiefly role than other chiefs, viewed Judge Newman—”The Great Dissenter” of the Federal Circuit—as disrupting the order of what Moore views as her court. And there’s now empirical evidence showing that since Newman’s departure, dissent rates on the court have “collapse[d],” and “[t]he Federal Circuit has become, in the space of two years, one of the most consensus-oriented appellate courts in the federal system” (in the words of Professor Dennis Crouch of Patently-O). Mission accomplished?

Judges in Los Angeles County Superior Court, perhaps the busiest trial court in the country, are now using an AI tool called Learned Hand—great name—to help with their heavy workloads. Responding to concerns about AI fails (which OJ readers never get tired of reading about), Learned Hand’s CEO, Shlomo Klapper, pointed out that it has a proprietary tool for verifying citations and avoiding hallucinations (similar to the ones offered by BriefCatch and Clearbrief).

In nominations news, last week brought no new nominations or confirmations. But the Senate Judiciary Committee held confirmation hearings for four nominees—Kathleen “Katie” Lane (D. Mont.), Sheria Clarke (D.S.C.), Evan Rikhye (D.V.I.), and Kara Westercamp (C.I.T.)—and things got testy at times. Lane, who graduated from law school in 2017, had to field questions about her lack of trial experience; Westercamp, who currently works in the Trump White House, “sincerely apologize[d]” for tweets attacking senators (including Republican ones). But my guess is that they’ll all ultimately win approval from the SJC and get confirmed by the full Senate.

In memoriam:

New York State Supreme Court Justice William Erlbaum, who served in the New York state courts since 1979, passed away at 89.

Judge James Harberson—who served as a city judge in Watertown, New York, from 1986 to 2011—passed away at 85.

May they rest in peace.

Job of the Week: an opportunity for a midlevel real estate associate in Atlanta.

Lateral Link is partnering with an Am Law 100 firm seeking a midlevel associate to join its top-ranked real estate practice in Atlanta. This is a compelling opportunity to work on sophisticated commercial transactions within a nationally recognized, Chambers-ranked practice. The role offers exposure to acquisitions, dispositions, financings, joint ventures, development, and leasing across a range of asset classes. Ideal candidates will have at least three years of Biglaw experience, strong academic credentials, and the ability to manage complex, client-facing matters in a collaborative environment. If interested, please send your résumé and law school transcript to Marion Wilson at mwilson@laterallink.com.

Rulings of the Week: State of New Mexico v. Meta Platforms, Inc. and K.G.M. v. Meta.

My husband Zach and I disagree on social media. He sees it as the devil’s handiwork, as well as a harbinger of doom; I acknowledge its downsides and dangers, but still view it as a net positive—for both me, professionally and personally, and for civilization writ large. (We feel pretty much the exact same way about AI.)

Last week, juries in New Mexico and California state courts decided two high-profile cases that could be viewed as big-picture judgments about social media. On Tuesday, in a case brought by New Mexico Attorney General Raúl Torrez, a jury in Santa Fe issued a $375 million verdict against Meta, ordering the company to pay that amount in damages for violating state consumer protection laws.

Then on Wednesday, in a case brought by a now 20-year-old woman identified as K.G.M., a jury in Los Angeles issued a verdict against Meta and YouTube, ordering them to pay $4.2 million and $1.8 million, respectively, in combined compensatory and punitive damages. This litigation, a personal-injury case brought by a private plaintiff, involved claims of negligence and failure to warn.

Mark Lanier, one of the lawyers representing K.G.M., described the event as “a righteous event and a righteous moment.” And in light of the anger and backlash directed today at social-media and technology companies, who are blamed for any number of societal ills, I suspect many Americans would agree with Lanier.

But there could be another side here. As Ari Cohn of the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression wrote on FIRE’s website, in an essay titled “The Big Tech verdicts you’re cheering for are actually terrible for free speech,” social-media platforms and the ideas and information they transmit “aren’t tangible items that inherently and invariably have physical impacts on the human body.” And “the minute we start treating speech as if it were just another physical product is the minute we hand the government the power to decide what we can read, watch, and say. That’s dangerous—and the First Amendment forbids it.”

What comes next? As legal experts told The Times (gift link), it’s too early to know what to make of these cases, the first of many state and federal lawsuits filed against the social-media companies to result in jury verdicts. Meta and YouTube plan to appeal—and Professor Stephen Carter, in a Bloomberg column, expressed skepticism that the verdicts will be upheld.

Carter also made a larger point: “Social media takes it on the chin for lots of things these days…. Maybe the claims are all bunk. Maybe some or even most of the blame is deserved. Either way, I think the courtroom isn’t the forum to resolve them.” Put another way, we as a nation need to figure out how we feel about social media—and find a sensible way to reflect our views in law and policy.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

The Supreme Court issued opinions in Zorn v. Linton (a summary reversal of the Second Circuit in an excessive force/qualified immunity case); Cox Communications, Inc. v. Sony Music Entertainment (holding that a cable company isn’t liable for copyright infringement simply because it provides a service to the public knowing that some customers will infringe copyrights); and Rico v. United States (concerning whether the Sentencing Reform Act authorizes a rule automatically extending a defendant’s term of supervised release when the defendant absconds). For more coverage, I refer you to SCOTUSblog, which has returned to providing coverage of every merits case at the Court. (And speaking of SCOTUSblog, please join us this Wednesday, April 1, at 9:30 a.m., for live blogging of the oral argument in the birthright citizenship case.)

Villarreal v. Alaniz. Justice Sonia Sotomayor dissented from the denial of certiorari in this First Amendment case, brought by FIRE on behalf of citizen journalist Priscilla Villarreal. As Justice Sotomayor put it, Villarreal “was arrested for doing something journalists do every day: posing questions to a public official…. It should be obvious that this arrest violated the First Amendment.”

Hellman v. Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education. From Judge Gustavo Gelpí‘s opinion: “Massachusetts grants all of the state's students with disabilities an individual entitlement to publicly funded special education services. But state regulations distinguish between public and private school students when determining where those services may be provided. While public school students may generally receive services at their schools of enrollment, private school students may receive services only at a public school or another public or neutral location.” Parents with children in private school argued that this policy unconstitutionally burdened their fundamental right to choose private school for their kids, but the First Circuit disagreed.

Petersen Energía Inversora, S.A.U. v. Argentine Republic. In an opinion by Judge Denny Chin, a divided panel of the Second Circuit reversed the district court’s $16.1 billion judgment in favor of minority shareholders of YPF, the Argentine oil and gas company that Argentina (re)nationalized in 2012. Judge José Cabranes dissented. (Disclosure: This ruling was bad news for Burford Capital, which stood to benefit from the judgment, and Burford is a sponsor of Original Jurisdiction.)

United States v. Williamson. A panel of the Fifth Circuit rejected a defendant’s challenge to his conviction under 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(1)—which criminalizes possession of a firearm by a convicted felon—based on circuit precedent holding that § 922(g)(1) doesn’t violate the Commerce Clause. But Judge Don Willett wrote a thoughtful concurrence, joined by Judge Cory Wilson, expressing doubts about the correctness of that precedent.

Avila v. Bondi. Agreeing with the Fifth Circuit, a divided panel of the Eighth Circuit held, in an opinion by Judge Bobby Shepherd, that the Trump administration can arrest undocumented immigrants inside the U.S. and hold them without bond. Judge Ralph Erickson dissented, noting that “[f]ive presidential administrations, including the first Trump administration, and most immigration judges” interpreted the statute at issue contrary to the administration’s current view.

Woolard v. Thurmond. From John Ross’s (delightfully concise) description of this case for Short Circuit: “in en banc news, the Ninth Circuit (over dissentals) will not reconsider its decision that California parents who homeschool their kids—by means of enrolling them in a public charter school program that pays for educational materials—cannot have the state pay for religious educational materials.” Judge Patrick Bumatay wrote one dissent from the denial of rehearing, which four of his colleagues joined, and Judge Lawrence VanDyke wrote the other dissental, which two other judges joined. (This was not a case about “swinging dicks,” and the opinions were appropriate for the whole family.)

Anthropic PBC v. U.S. Department of War. Judge Rita Lin (N.D. Cal.) temporarily blocked the Pentagon’s designation of Anthropic as a national security supply-chain risk (but stayed her ruling to give the administration time to appeal).

Motsinger v. Cosby. A jury in Los Angeles Superior Court awarded $59.25 million in damages to Donna Motsinger, a former waitress who accused disgraced actor and comedian Bill Cosby of drugging and sexually assaulting her.

Litigation of the Week: Watson v. Republican National Committee.

Last Monday, the U.S. Supreme Court heard oral argument in Watson v. Republican National Committee (RNC). Watson concerns whether federal election-day statutes preempt state laws that allow the counting of ballots that are cast on or before Election Day, but not received by election officials until sometime after that day.

Watson challenges a Mississippi law that permits mail-in ballots to be counted as long as they’re postmarked by, then received within five business days of, Election Day. But as noted by Amy Howe of SCOTUSblog, “more than a dozen states have similar laws, [so] the Court’s ruling—which is expected by late June or early July—could have significant implications for federal elections, beginning as soon as November.”

What’s the likely outcome? According to Howe, as well as most of the commentaries collected at How Appealing, it appears the Court will reject the Mississippi law—i.e., a majority of justices will hold that ballots must be received on or before Election Day in order to count—and some state officials are already preparing for that outcome. For more on Watson, I refer you to Sarah Isgur and David French of Advisory Opinions.

In other litigation news:

The quartet of law firms targeted by executive orders from the Trump administration—Jenner & Block, Perkins Coie, Susman Godfrey, and WilmerHale—came out swinging in their D.C. Circuit briefs, opposing the administration’s appeals of its four defeats in the district courts.

Barry J. Pollack, counsel to former Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro, claims that the Trump administration is preventing his client from accessing the money he needs to fund his legal defense—and while Judge Alvin Hellerstein (S.D.N.Y.) declined to dismiss the criminal case against Maduro for that reason, His Honor expressed displeasure with the administration’s actions.

The Roman Catholic Diocese of Albany reached a $148 million settlement with hundreds of victims of child sexual abuse (who could sue thanks to a 2019 New York State law, the Child Victims Act, that allowed adults who were sexually abused as children to file claims that were previously time-barred).

Represented by Boies Schiller Flexner and Edwards Henderson, Jeffrey Epstein victims secured a $72.5 million settlement from Bank of America, which allegedly facilitated his sex-trafficking crimes by providing him with banking services (after JPMorgan Chase cut him off).

Joshua Spriestersbach, 54, will receive a $975,000 settlement from the city and county of Honolulu, based on how he was wrongfully arrested and held for more than two years at a psychiatric hospital in Hawaii. He might also get a $200,000 settlement from the state of Hawaii—whose public defender’s office allegedly contributed to the situation. It’s a pretty crazy story (gift link).

Deal of the Week: the $22 billion merger of Corebridge Financial and Equitable Holdings.

Two financial-services firms, Corebridge Financial and Equitable Holdings, announced their plan to combine through a $22 billion merger, which they touted as creating a leader in the retirement, life insurance, and asset management industries. The law firms on the deal are Skadden Arps for Corebridge and Paul Weiss for Equitable.

In non-M&A deal news, the legal AI giant Harvey completed another funding round, raising $200 million—at a total valuation of $11 billion. While it’s certainly possible we’re going through an AI bubble, it’s not quite like the dot-com bubble of the late 1990s, when companies with little to no revenue or customers received ridiculous valuations. Take Harvey: it boasts many paying clients—like my latest podcast guest Jed Bergman, who said that he and his colleagues at Glenn Agre Bergman & Fuentes are always looking for new use cases—and Harvey’s annual revenue is “significantly north of $200 million,” according to CEO Winston Weinberg.

Law Firm of the Week: Freshfields.

For most of my years covering Biglaw, the so-called “Magic Circle” firms—yes, I know they don’t love that moniker, but it’s useful—struggled to gain traction in the U.S. legal market. Despite their undeniable prestige, impeccable pedigrees, and incredible global reach, these U.K.-founded firms couldn’t seem to succeed on our shores, even as they thrived in continental Europe, Asia, and other markets.

But at some point in the past decade, that changed—and now these firms are major players in the States. Case in point: Freshfields.

The firm was involved in at least two billion-dollar deals announced last week. The smaller transaction, the French food conglomerate Danone’s $1.2 billion acquisition of the British meal-replacement brand Huel, was a classic Magic Circle deal: a cross-border transaction with at least one U.K. party. But Freshfields is also advising Terns Pharmaceuticals, a California-based biotech company, in its $6.7 billion sale to Merck—a purely domestic deal, and one that any number of Am Law 100 firms would love to be involved with. (Covington & Burling represents Merck.)

What allowed Freshfields to take its U.S. practice to the next level? A key factor was its willingness to pay top dollar for top talent, a step the Magic Circle firms had been unwilling to take for years—which brings us to the next big news out of Freshfields.

As reported by Bloomberg Law, Freshfields overhauled its partner compensation system, and now its U.S. partners can earn as much as $17 million a year. In addition, it added a non-equity tier to its partnership—a step taken by many other firms seeking greater flexibility in how they pay their partners, especially the biggest rainmakers.

To be sure, $17 million is far from the top of the market in partner pay—and I wouldn’t be surprised if Freshfields ends up having to cough up more, at least if it wants to hang on to high-profile hires like Ethan Klingsberg (from Cleary) and Damien Zoubek and Jenny Hochenberg (from Cravath—and, by the way, the lead partners on the Merck/Terns deal). For now, however, a willingness to pay partners north of $15 million—even if it might get a few noses out of joint back in London—makes clear that Freshfields is selling top legal talent (and not organic milk at Whole Foods).

In other firm news:

Speaking of being willing to pay up for top talent, Susman Godfrey announced enhanced clerkship bonuses (as one excited reader related to me): $180,000 for one federal district or appellate clerkship, and $200,000 for two or more clerkships. In addition, the firm announced what it described as an “applicant-first summer program,” which you can read about in this press release. It’s similar to that of Munger Tolles & Olson, which I previously praised—and I’m a fan of Susman’s new approach, for all the same reasons.

Speaking of elite litigation boutiques, this year Wilkinson Stekloff celebrated its tenth anniversary—and was the subject of two glowing profiles, in Bloomberg Law and The American Lawyer.

I don’t know what to make of this lawsuit filed in New York state court against White & Case, making claims of discrimination, retaliation, and a hostile work environment. The anonymous plaintiff, who worked for the firm as a digital production specialist, alleges in his complaint that he “was stripped naked and photographed by his supervisor, and potentially other colleagues, while unconscious during a firm retreat”—and that the resulting photographs were then circulated within the firm. A White & Case representative told Law.com that “these claims are baseless, and we intend to defend them vigorously through the appropriate process.” It will be interesting to see how this turns out: on the one hand, the allegations are hard to believe, and the firm’s denial is aggressive; on the other hand, there should be forensic evidence to support them if true (assuming it hasn’t been destroyed, a possibility raised by the plaintiff’s counsel, Eric Baum of Eisenberg & Baum).

Taylor Duma, founded in Atlanta in 2005, will be closing its doors. It grew to more than 150 lawyers as of 2023—but after key partners defected over the past two years, the remaining partners made “the very hard decision to shut down the firm,” as managing partner Marc Taylor told Law.com.

Move of the Week: the NAACP hiring Kristen Clarke as its general counsel.

Kristen Clarke, former head of the DOJ Civil Rights Division in the Biden administration, is no stranger to controversy. But you know who’s also not afraid to ruffle some feathers? The NAACP, which describes itself as “the home of grassroots activism for civil rights and social justice,” prepared to “advocate, agitate, and litigate for the civil rights due to Black America.”

So I wasn’t surprised when the NAACP, one of the nation’s oldest and most well-known civil-rights groups, announced its hiring of Clarke as general counsel (succeeding Janette McCarthy Wallace, who will remain at the organization but transition into a special-counsel role). In her new role, Clarke will oversee NAACP strategy and operations, and she will also head up its efforts on issues like voter access, gerrymandering, and the First Amendment.

Other notable moves:

In New York, HSF Kramer brought over a trio of M&A partners—Robert Leung, Mike Huang, and Daniel Grossman—from Paul Hastings. They had been at Paul Hastings since March 2017, after joining from Boies Schiller Flexner (when the litigation-focused firm spun off what had been its small corporate practice, a move I wrote about in Above the Law).

Down in D.C., another group of three partners—litigators Archis Parasharami, Daniel Jones, and Kevin Ranlett—moved from Mayer Brown to Skadden Arps. Their practice focuses on class actions and mass arbitrations.

Staying in the nation’s capital, Greenberg Traurig hired former Maryland attorney general Douglas Gansler as co-chair of its nationwide state attorneys general practice. He came over from Cadwalader, along with Stephen Ruckman (with whom he worked when he was Maryland AG).

Back in the Big Apple, Allison Liff, who co-led the leveraged finance practice at Freshfields, jumped to Ropes & Gray.

And then we came to the end—of another edition of Judicial Notice. I wish you a good week, as well as a happy Passover and Easter (in advance, as applicable). And to my fellow denizens of the New York tri-state area, where we have some rainy days ahead, stay dry—and always carry an umbrella, even if it’s not cloudy when you leave home.
items.description: I spent last week trying to get back into the swing of things after vacation. It was a low-key week—and after a hectic start to 2026, I welcomed it. Even after a productive week, though, I’m still playing catch-up on many things in my life, both professional and personal—and as a result, I’m doing…

items.title: Judicial Notice (03.22.26): The $10 Billion Law Firm
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

Burford Capital helps companies and law firms unlock the value of their legal assets. With a portfolio of over $7 billion and listings on the NYSE and LSE, Burford provides capital to finance high-value commercial litigation and arbitration—without adding cost or risk or giving up control. Clients include Fortune 500 companies and Am Law 100 firms, who turn to Burford to pursue strong claims, manage legal costs and accelerate recoveries. Learn more at burfordcapital.com.

I wrote much of this edition of Judicial Notice while on our flight back home from spring break in Costa Rica yesterday. We had a great time; after a few days at the beach in Guanacaste, we headed to the La Fortuna and Arenal area, where we enjoyed the hot springs, did some hiking, and saw plenty of wildlife. As usual after a vacation, our main challenge now is getting back into the work and school mindset.

We did deal with some work while away. On Tuesday, I participated in the (excellent) New York City Bar Association webinar about (insanely early) Biglaw recruiting of law students. And on Friday, Zach and I hopped online for the SCOTUSblog live blog (although it wasn’t terribly exciting—with all due respect to Justice Elena Kagan, author of the Supreme Court’s unanimous opinion in Olivier v. City of Brandon).

Now, on to the news.

Lawyer of the Week: Deborah Leslie.

In recent weeks, I’ve written about a (now former) federal prosecutor who found himself in hot water after possibly using AI to draft a court filing. The same problem might have affected a state prosecutor—and the moment went viral.

Unfortunately for Deborah Leslie of the Clayton County District Attorney's Office, she got called out by Chief Justice Nels Peterson of the Georgia Supreme Court, for a filing that contained nonexistent cases. The exchange was caught on courtroom cameras and posted to X by Anna Bower—where it has, as of this writing, been viewed 5 million times.

The offending filing was a draft order denying a new trial that was filed by the prosecution and adopted by the trial court. At oral argument, Chief Justice Peterson pointed out to Leslie that the trial court’s order denying the defendant’s motion for a new trial, which was based on the prosecution’s proposed order, contained at least five citations to nonexistent cases, at least five citations to cases that didn’t support the proposition for which they were cited, and three nonexistent quotations.

What did Leslie have to say for herself? She acknowledged preparing a draft order, but said it was revised by the court prior to issuance—i.e., she threw the trial judge, Judge Jewel Scott, under the bus. But Chief Justice Peterson, who had clearly done his homework, immediately pointed out that “[t]hose nonexistent cases were cited in your initial brief opposing the motion for a new trial.” After rifling through her papers at the lectern, Leslie said, “I’m not aware of that, but I would be glad to research and provide the court with a supplement.”

Don’t worry about it, Ms. Leslie; Court TV did your work for you (much like an AI tool). After comparing the 37-page proposed order prepared by the state with the 33-page order ultimately issued by Judge Scott, Court TV found that the citations lined up. So Leslie can’t get out of this pickle by blaming the judge (although as Professor Carissa Byrne Hessick pointed out, Judge Scott isn’t blameless here either, since she apparently adopted the prosecution’s citations without verifying them independently).

In an age where you can use technological tools to catch hallucinated cases, AI fails are increasingly difficult to defend. But they will continue to happen, because AI assistants are not infallible. So if you make such a mistake, don’t deny or deflect; instead, acknowledge it openly and apologize (as did this Biglaw partner—and as did I, after using an embarrassing AI-generated image that I didn’t bother to review).

Other lawyers in the news:

If you need something from the Trump administration, your first phone call should be to Mike Davis. He claims to be “the best fixer in Washington, period”—and based on the reporting of The Wall Street Journal (gift link), he seems to have the track record to back this claim up.

In the wake of the many lawyers who have quit their jobs with the federal government (or have been fired), the Justice Department needs more than a few good men (and women). So the DOJ is waiving its longstanding policy requiring newly hired federal prosecutors to have at least one year of experience.

Speaking of lawyers leaving the Trump administration, Margaret “Meg” Ryan resigned as director of the enforcement division of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). She stepped down after only six months in the position, without giving a reason; if you have info as to why, feel free to drop me a line.

In memoriam:

Robert S. Mueller III—who served as director of the FBI for 12 years, then later as the special counsel who investigated Russian interference in the 2016 presidential election—passed away at 81. He had a long and distinguished legal career that included, in addition to his most high-profile roles, service as a federal prosecutor in San Francisco and Washington, D.C.; leadership of the Criminal Division at the DOJ; and multiple stints in private practice at WilmerHale. I had the privilege and pleasure of meeting Bob Mueller, at a dinner in 2014, and came away impressed. (For a photo of me holding forth while Mueller listened intently—or at least pretended to)—see this LinkedIn post by Bob Ambrogi.)

John J. Park, Jr.—who spent much of his legal career in public and government service, including with the U.S. Army JAG Corps and the Alabama Attorney General’s Office—passed awayat 71.

May they rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Chief Justice John Roberts.

Is Chief Justice John Roberts like Obi-Wan Kenobi? Does he grow more powerful the more you strike him down?

Adam Liptak—writing in The Docket, his legal newsletter for The New York Times (gift link)—highlighted a Truth Social diatribe that Donald Trump issued against the Court last Sunday. It amounted to an extended diatribe about the justices for voting against Trump’s interests in the 2020 election and again in the recent tariffs case—but in Liptak’s view, it amounted to “a gift” to the Chief and his fellow justices, since it “inadvertently made the case for the Court’s independence.”

In terms of his official title, Chief Justice Roberts is actually the chief justice not of the Supreme Court, but of the United States. As the leader of the entire federal judiciary, his job includes protecting judges from unwarranted hostility and threats—which are sadly on the rise, as five lower-court judges discussed at a virtual event.

During a Tuesday event at Rice University, Judge Lee Rosenthal (S.D. Tex.) asked Chief Justice Roberts how he deals with criticism. After noting that it comes with the job if you’re a judge, he added that sometimes “the criticism can move from the focus on legal analysis to personalities.” Such “personally directed hostility,” according to the Chief, “is dangerous—and it’s got to stop.” Chief Justice Roberts’s remarks generated extensive media coverage, collected at How Appealing by Howard Bashman (along with video—the key discussion begins around the 23-minute mark).

The Supreme Court has received criticism for not being sufficiently supportive of lower-court judges, especially when they rule against the Trump administration. So I think the Chief’s remarks—which didn’t call out Trump by name, but clearly implicate him and his supporters—are noteworthy. As Judge Rosenthal put it, “On behalf of trial judges everywhere, I want to personally thank you because while we know that you may not always agree with us, we always know that you have our backs, and that means a great deal. So, thank you—and I hope it continues.”

In other news about judges and the judiciary:

Sorry, he’s not sorry: in a speech delivered after his controversial “swinging dicks” dissent, Judge Lawrence VanDyke argued that his Ninth Circuit colleagues are the ones who should apologize, for “routinely impos[ing] their personal policy preferences on the law.” He then offered them a deal: “You stop breaking the norm of how our system was designed to work, and I’ll stop breaking those norms that promote collegiality and the perception of legitimacy.” For anyone who isn’t tired of this story—i.e., can’t get enough “dick,” à la Blanche Devereaux of The Golden Girls—check out Sarah Isgur and David French’s discussion on Advisory Opinions. (And for anyone who skimmed my story about that dissent too quickly, please note that this sentence was a joke: “Not to be outdone, Judge James Ho subsequently issued a Fifth Circuit opinion that opened as follows: ‘This is a case about huge, throbbing cocks.’”)

Judge Zahid Quraishi (D.N.J.) is quite upset over the leadership structure at my former workplace, the U.S. Attorney’s Office in New Jersey, currently being run by a “triumvirate” after the departure of Alina Habba—and he took it out on two assistant U.S. attorneys, Daniel Rosenblum and Mark Coyne, who appeared before him for a sentencing. After reading the transcript, my own view is that Judge Quraishi was unnecessarily harsh toward the two AUSAs, who didn’t come up with this (admittedly bizarre) scheme but are stuck defending it. (Disclosure: I’m not unbiased, since Mark Coyne is a friend and former colleague of mine.)

In nominations news, the Senate confirmed Anna St. John to serve as a judge on the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Louisiana, by a vote of 51-45. St. John currently serves as president and general counsel of the Hamilton Lincoln Law Institute, a nonprofit public-interest firm (mentioned in last week’s Judicial Notice for filing a proposed class action on behalf of a putative class of drivers who got stuck for hours in a massive traffic jam caused by a pro-Palestinian protest).

In memoriam: Judge E. Grady Jolly, who served on the Fifth Circuit for more than 40 years, passed away at 88. For personal reflections on Judge Jolly from a former clerk, see Ilya Shapiro’s tribute over at City Journal.

Job of the Week: an opportunity for an appellate litigator in Houston.

Lateral Link is working exclusively with the leader of a Chambers-ranked appellate group in Houston seeking a strong associate to join its team. This is a rare opportunity to join a highly respected, close-knit practice where associates take on meaningful responsibility early and work directly with partners on complex, high-level matters. The group is known for its collegial, mentorship-driven culture, offering a clear path to partnership. They seek an associate with two to seven years of experience, a federal trial- or appellate-court clerkship, outstanding academic credentials, and excellent research and writing skills. If you’re interested—or even just curious—please contact Wendy Boone at wendyboone@laterallink.com.

Rulings of the Week: Ford v. McKesson and United States v. Burger.

Last week, I highlighted a trio of Ninth Circuit rulings in First Amendment cases that generally came down on the side of free speech (and please note the update I appended to my discussion of NetChoice, LLC v. Bonta). But this week, I’m featuring a pair of Fifth Circuit rulings, issued on the same day, in which the First Amendment claims did not prevail:

Ford v. McKesson. In this long-running litigation filed by John Ford, a Baton Rouge police officer, against DeRay McKesson, a Black Lives Matter activist, a divided panel of the Fifth Circuit ruled that after eight years of legal sparring, “[i]t is time for Officer Ford to have a jury assess his claim that DeRay Mckesson’s negligence in leading a violent protest caused him to suffer injuries at the hands of rioters.” Judge Edith Jones wrote the majority opinion; Judge Carolyn Dineen King dissented, arguing that the decision “imperils First Amendment liberties.”

United States v. Burger. In January 2025, a federal grand jury indicted James Wesley Burger, an 18-year-old high school senior at the time, on three counts of transmitting threats in interstate commerce. But he didn’t make these alleged threats in person or over the phone; instead, he made them to other users of the game Roblox. The district court dismissed the indictment, concluding that no reasonable juror could find that Burger’s statements were “true threats” outside the protection of the First Amendment. But in a unanimous, per curiam opinion, the Fifth Circuit reversed, holding that “a trial on the merits is necessary to evaluate the sufficiency of the government’s evidence.”

The Fifth Circuit is the most conservative circuit court in the country, and this sometimes manifests itself in decisions that could be construed as against free speech (although note that some of the court’s conservative members, including Judges Jim Ho and Don Willett, are some of the strongest defenders of the First Amendment). I also think it’s fair to say that Ford and Burger might have come out differently if they had been litigated in another, more liberal circuit court.

But note a common theme tying together Ford and Burger: they could be viewed less as cases against free speech and more as cases in favor of letting juries decide. In these two litigations, context matters—the context of the protest, the context of the video game—and jurors might be better situated than judges to make the required calls. So let’s see how the trials turn out before fearing for the First Amendment.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

State of New York v. Trump. From John Ross of Short Circuit: “Within days of President Trump beginning his second term, OMB orders a sweeping freeze to all federal financial assistance programs. The following day, 22 states sue, alleging that the freeze is unlawful. The following day, OMB rescinds the memo announcing the policy. Feds: So the lawsuit is moot. First Circuit [by Chief Judge David Barron]: Then why did the White House Press Secretary post, ‘This is NOT a rescission of the federal funding freeze. It is simply a rescission of the OMB memo’? The case is live and the preliminary injunction against the freeze is affirmed. But the part of the injunction actually requiring disbursement of the money is vacated.”

In terms of Trump-related litigation in the district courts:

Judge Brian Murphy (D. Mass.) blocked the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) from implementing certain vaccine-related policies promulgated by Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

Speaking of Secretary Kennedy, Judge Mustafa Kasubhai (D. Or.) blocked HHS’s effort to restrict healthcare providers from providing gender-affirming care to minors.

Judge Paul L. Friedman (D.D.C.) held unconstitutional the Trump administration’s new restrictions on media reporters and news organizations covering the Pentagon—and noted that “in light of the country’s recent incursion into Venezuela and its ongoing war with Iran, it is more important than ever that the public have access to information from a variety of perspectives about what its government is doing.”

Staying on the media beat, Judge Royce Lamberth (D.D.C.) ruled that the administration’s near shutdown of Voice of America was illegal—and ordered the reinstatement of more than 1,000 people who were placed on leave at the government-funded broadcaster.

On Friday, a federal jury in San Francisco (N.D. Cal.) found Elon Musk liable for some losses experienced by Twitter investors, based on comments he posted on social media threatening to walk away from his purchase of the company. Damages remain to be determined, but lawyers for the plaintiff investors claim that Musk might have to pay former Twitter shareholders up to $2.5 billion.

Whenever you see a giant jury verdict, take it with a grain of salt, at least until all post-trial motions and appeals have been resolved. Los Angeles County Superior Court Judge Ruth Kwan granted Johnson & Johnson’s motion to set aside an award of $950 million in punitive damages to the family of a woman who died from mesothelioma, which they claimed was caused by J&J’s talc products.

Turning to the state courts, my favorite verdict of last week came in Shawn D. Cooley v. John Edgar Foreman aka Afroman. In 2022, police officers in southern Ohio raided Afroman’s home, investigating (false) tips of kidnapping and drug trafficking. The police found no evidence of such crimes and criminal charges were never brought, but the officers did cause significant damage to Afroman’s property. He did what you’d expect a rapper to do: he made multiple (humorous) songs about the raid, using real video from the raid (caught on his home security cameras), and he mocked the officers mercilessly, calling them out by name. They sued him, claiming defamation and privacy violations. But they had a weak case, as explained by Aaron Terr of the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), and a jury ruled entirely in Afroman’s favor last Wednesday. For more on the case, read this tweet, watch this video, or read this Times article.

Litigations of the Week: Noem v. Doe and Trump v. Miot.

Regular readers of Judicial Notice are aware of the numerous cases bouncing around the federal courts relating to Temporary Protected Status (TPS). The TPS program allows non-citizens from designated countries to live temporarily in the United States

The Trump administration has been trying to end TPS as to a slew of different countries, but lower-court judges have frequently ruled to prevent the government from ending TPS. As discussed last week, the federal government recently requested interim relief from the Court, asking the justices to allow it to end TPS for immigrants from Haiti, and Solicitor General John Sauer asked the Court to grant certiorari before judgment and reach the merits—to end what he called the “unsustainable cycle” of SCOTUS giving the green light to TPS terminations, followed by lower-court judges finding other grounds for preserving TPS status.

Last Monday, the Court granted cert before judgment in cases involving TPS for Syrian and Haitian nationals. The justices will hear argument in Noem v. Doe (Syria) and Trump v. Miot (Haiti) in April, with a ruling likely to follow near the end of the Court’s Term, sometime in late June or early July. For more on this incredibly important litigation—which affects hundreds of thousands of individuals and implicates important legal issues—check out Advisory Opinions.

In other litigation news:

The D.C. Circuit granted the Trump administration’s motion to withdraw its motion to voluntarily dismiss its appeals in the Biglaw executive-order cases—i.e., to keep on defending the EOs in court.

Twenty-three states and 14 cities and counties sued the administration, challenging its decision to revoke the federal government’s “endangerment finding”—its 2009 conclusion that climate change is a serious threat.

Two fired F.B.I. agents sued the FBI, FBI Director Kash Patel, the DOJ, and Attorney General Pam Bondi, challenging the legality of their firings.

The DOJ filed another lawsuit against Harvard, alleging that the university remains “deliberately indifferent” to harassment of Jewish and Israeli students.

The DOJ moved to dismiss the criminal case against two former police officers in Louisville, Kentucky, who allegedly falsified the search warrant that ultimately led to the fatal shooting of Breonna Taylor.

Former New Jersey attorney general Matt Platkin, now in private practice, told me in our recent podcast interview that he and his colleagues at Platkin LLP were working on a number of socially significant lawsuits. Earlier this month, they sued Open AI, alleging that ChatGPT is an addictive and dangerous product.

Deal of the Week: Nexstar’s $6.2 billion acquisition of Tegna.

On Thursday, after receiving approval from the Federal Communications Commission, Nexstar Media closed its $6.2 billion acquisition of a rival TV company, Tegna. The post-merger entity oversees around 265 television stations in 44 states, making it one of the nation’s biggest owners of local television stations.

The law firms on the deal are Kirkland & Ellis, Wiley Rein, and Morrison Foerster, representing Nexstar, and Wachtell Lipton and Covington Burling, counseling Tegna. And even though the transaction closed, the lawyers might still have more work ahead of them: last Friday, eight states moved for a temporary restraining order to block Nexstar and Tegna from proceeding with their integration, based on antitrust grounds—specifically, too much concentration in certain local TV markets.

Coming on the heels of dozens of states continuing to prosecute the antitrust case against LiveNation (discussed in last week’s JN), the effort to block the Nexstar/Tegna merger is another reminder that the federal government isn’t the only game in town when it comes to antitrust enforcement. But whether states can be as effective as the feds in this area remains to be seen.

Law Firm of the Week: Kirkland & Ellis.

Thanks in large part to its work advising on billion-dollar deals like Nexstar/Tegna, Kirkland & Ellis set another record. Based on its 2025 financial results, K&E became the first law firm in history to hit more than $10 billion in annual revenue—and its equity partners earned more than $11 million in profits per equity partner.

Around $3 billion of that revenue comes from the litigation side, which means that the bulk of Kirkland’s revenue comes from its transactional work. As I’ve discussed in these pages before, K&E has evolved from being a litigation-focused firm to a deal-driven one, with a particular emphasis on work for private-equity clients.

As a result of this shift, Kirkland seems to have less tolerance for litigators who might generate controversy—even if those litigators might be extremely successful partners with large practices. In 2022, for example, Paul Clement and Erin Murphy departed from K&E and launched Clement & Murphy, after their work on Second Amendment cases rubbed some of Kirkland’s corporate clients the wrong way.

The latest heavy-hitting litigator to leave Kirkland is Jay Lefkowitz, who’s departing from the firm after more than 30 years. He’s not moving to another firm or going in-house; instead, he said that he’ll be transitioning into “teaching, working with non-profit organizations. and helping some exciting young companies.”

What’s prompting Lefkowitz’s departure? Here’s one possibility: in 2007, he was part of a legal team that represented Jeffrey Epstein, obtaining what was widely viewed as a sweetheart deal for some of the late financier’s sex crimes. And the two men interacted outside the context of Lefkowitz’s legal representation: as reported by The Financial Times, Lefkowitz invited Epstein to his son’s bar mitzvah, and Lefkowitz also asked to borrow Epstein’s helicopter for a trip to the Hamptons.

When asked by Patrick Smith of The American Lawyer about whether his retirement from Kirkland might be related to his work for Epstein, Lefkowitz responded with a statement: “In 2007, I represented Epstein, like every one of my clients, as effectively as I could. Although I believe everyone is entitled to representation no matter how heinous their crimes, knowing what we all know now, I would not have taken on the matter.” As for Kirkland, a firm spokesperson stated, “To say the least, we deeply regret the firm’s 2007 representation of Jeffrey Epstein. It was clearly a mistake to take that on and, without question, we would not have made the same decision today.”

In other news of departures from K&E, partners are starting to follow David Nemecek, a leading figure in the liability-management space, over to his new home, Simpson Thacher. They include restructuring partners Brian Schartz and John Luze, as reported by The WSJ, and restructuring partner Christine Bae and finance partner Jacob Ruby, per Law.com.

Move of the Week: Manatt hiring entertainment lawyers Ellie Heisler, Jessica Boardman, and Mike Lawhead.

Since the Oscars just took place, it seems fitting to feature showbiz lawyers as the stars of the latest Move of the Week. As reported by Variety and Law360, three entertainment partners—Ellie Heisler, Jessica Boardman, and Mike Lawhead—joined the Los Angeles office of Manatt Phelps & Phillips. Heisler, Lawhead (great name for an attorney), and associate April Hua joined from Nixon Peabody; Boardman came from an entertainment-focused boutique, Myman Greenspan.

Other notable moves:

Not too far away (depending on L.A. traffic), Goodwin Procter opened an Orange County office in Newport Beach. The O.C. outpost will be led by Richard Grabowski, who left Jones Day after 36 years, along with two other litigation partners, Ryan Ball and John Vogt.

Speaking of new offices, Reed Smith launched in Boston, its fourth new outpost in 14 months, with a dozen lawyers from seven different Am Law 50 firms—including Grant Butler, a Chambers-ranked regulatory lawyer focused on the financial industry, who came from K&L Gates.

Prominent trial lawyer Hildy Sastre moved to the Miami office of DLA Piper from Shook Hardy & Bacon, where she had co-chaired the product-liability practice.

Teal Luthy Miller, former acting U.S. attorney for the Western District of Washington, joined the Seattle office of Davis Wright Tremaine. (Fun fact: she’s married to Judge Eric Miller of the Ninth Circuit, whom she met when they were classmates at the University of Chicago Law School.)

Abbe Lowell, who has been extremely busy with his many Trump-related matters, hired his first partner at Lowell & Associates: former federal prosecutor Caleb Hayes-Deats, who came from MoloLamken.

Thanks for joining me for this edition of Judicial Notice. I’m glad to be back in the swing of things, after a lovely vacation, and I wish you all the best in the week to come.
